column5: Adds information about elevation properties of features in a Z-enabled feature class.
column7: The input features to process.
column11: The Z properties that will be added to the attribute table of the input feature class. The following  options are available:Z ĄŠSpot elevation of single-point feature.POINT_COUNT ĄŠNumber of points in each multipoint feature.Z_MIN ĄŠLowest Z value found in each multipoint, polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.Z_MAX ĄŠHighest Z value found in each multipoint, polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.Z_MEAN ĄŠAverage Z value  found in each multipoint, polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.LENGTH_3D ĄŠ3-dimensional length of each polyline or polygon feature. SURFACE_AREA ĄŠTotal area of the surface of a multipatch feature.VERTEX_COUNT ĄŠTotal number of vertices in each polyline or polygon feature.MIN_SLOPE ĄŠLowest slope value calculated for each polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.MAX_SLOPE ĄŠHighest slope value calculated for each polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.AVG_SLOPE ĄŠAverage slope value calculated for each polyline, polygon, or multipatch feature.VOLUME ĄŠVolume of space enclosed by each multipatch feature.
column15: An optional numeric value used to exclude portions of features from the resulting calculations. This can be useful when the 3D input contains relatively small features with extreme slopes which may  bias statistical results.  If the 3D inputs linear units are meters, specifying a value of 0.001 will result in the exclusion of lines or polygon edges that are shorter than 0.001 meters.  For multipatch features, the same value will result in the exclusion of its subparts that have an area less than 0.001 square meters. This parameter does not apply to point and multipoint features.  The Area filter is made available when the input is a multipatch, whereas the Length filter is provided when the input is a line or polygon.

column5: Creates a 3-dimensional buffer around points or lines to produce spherical or cylindrical multipatch features.
column7: The line or point features to be buffered.
column11: The output multipatch containing the 3D buffers.
column15: The distance of the buffer around the input features, which can be provided as either a linear distance or be derived from a numeric field in the input features attribute table. If the buffer distance is specified from an input field, its unit of measurement will be derived from the features spatial reference. If the linear distance is specified as a numeric value, the following units of measure are supported:UNKNOWNINCHESFEETYARDSMILESMILLIMETERSCENTIMETERSDECIMETERSMETERSKILOMETERS
column19: The shape of the buffer between the vertices of the line segments. This parameter is only valid for input line features.STRAIGHT ĄŠThe shape of connections  between vertices will be straight.    This is the default. ROUND ĄŠThe shape of connections  between vertices will be round.
column23: The number of segments used to represent the resulting multipatch features.   The default is 20, but any number between the range of 6 to 60 can be entered. A higher  Buffer Quality value produces smoother 3D features, but also lengthens the processing time.
column27: Simplifies the input lines by maintaining  their shape within the specified offset of its original form.  Simplification will not take place if no value is specified. The following units of measurement are supported:UNKNOWNINCHESFEETYARDSMILESMILLIMETERSCENTIMETERSDECIMETERSMETERSKILOMETERS

column5: Eliminates portions  of multipatch features in a target feature class that overlap with enclosed volumes of multipatch features in the subtraction feature class.
column7: The multipatch  features that will have its features removed by the subtrahend features.
column11: The multipatch features that will be subtracted from the input.
column15: The output multipatch feature class that will contain the resulting features.
column19: An optional table that stores information about the relationship between the input features and the difference output.  The following fields are present in this table:Output_IDĄŠThe ID of the output feature.Minuend_IDĄŠThe ID of the input feature.SubtrahendĄŠThe ID of the subtract feature.

column5: Creates  closed multipatch features in the output feature class using the features of the input multipatch.
column7: The multipatch features that will be used to construct closed multipatches.
column11: The output closed multipatch features.
column15: The resolution that will be used to construct the closed multipatch features.This value is defined using the linear units of the input features spatial reference.

column5: Creates 3D features using height values derived from the attribute of the input features.
column7: The features that will be used to create 3D features.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The field whose values will define the height of the resulting 3D features.
column19: An optional second height field used for lines. When using two height fields, each line will start at the first height and end at the second (sloped).

column5: Determines if 3D features from an input feature class are contained inside a closed multipatch, and writes an output table recording the features that are partially or fully inside the multipatch.
column7: The input multipatch or 3D point, line, or polygon feature class.
column11: The closed multipatch features that will be used as the containers for the input features.
column15: The output table providing a list of  3D Input Features that are inside or partially inside    the Input Multipatch Features which are closed.    The output table contains an OBJECTID (object ID), Target_ID, and Status field.  The Status field will state if the input feature (Target_ID) is inside or partially inside a  multipatch.
column19: Specifies if the output table will identify the relationship between the Input Features and the Input Multipatch Features through the creation of a Contain_ID field that identifies the multipatch feature that contains the input feature.CheckedĄŠThe multipatch feature that contains an input feature will be identified.UncheckedĄŠThe multipatch feature that contains an input feature will not be identified. This is the default.Specifies if the output table will identify the relationship between the Input Features and the Input Multipatch Features through the creation of a Contain_ID field that identifies the multipatch feature that contains the input feature.COMPLEX ĄŠThe multipatch feature that contains an input feature will be identified.SIMPLE ĄŠThe multipatch feature that contains an input feature will not be identified. This is the default.

column5: Computes the intersection of multipatch features to produce closed multipatches  encompassing the overlapping volumes, open multipatch features from the common surface areas, or lines from the intersecting edges.
column7: The multipatch features that will be intersected. When only one input feature layer or feature class is provided, the output will indicate the intersection of its own features.
column11: The optional second multipatch feature layer or feature class to be intersected with the first.
column15: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column19: Determines the type of intersection geometry created.SOLID ĄŠCreates a closed multipatch  representing the overlapping volumes between input features. This is the default.SURFACE ĄŠCreates a multipatch surface representing shared faces between input features. LINE ĄŠ Creates lines representing shared edges between input features.

column5: Returns the number of geometric intersections between  3D line and multipatch features and also provides optional features that represent points of intersection and also divide the 3D lines at such points.
column7: The line features that will be intersected with the multipatch features.
column11: The multipatch features that the lines will be intersected against.
column15: The input line feature attributes that will be stored with the optional output features. IDS_ONLY ĄŠ Only feature identification numbers will be stored. This is the default.ALL ĄŠAll attributes will be stored.
column19: Optional features that represent points of intersection between the 3D line and multipatch.
column23: Optional line features  that divide the input lines at each point of intersection with a multipatch feature.

column5: Evaluates multipatch features to determine whether each feature completely encloses a volume of space.
column7: The multipatch features to be tested.

column5: Calculates the three-dimensional distance from each input feature to the nearest feature that resides in one or more near feature classes.
column7: The input feature class whose features will be attributed with information about the nearest feature.
column11: The one or more features whose proximity to the input features will be calculated. If multiple feature classes are specified, an additional field named NEAR_FC will be added to the input feature class to identify which near feature class contained the closest feature.
column15: The maximum distance for which the nearest features from a given input will be determined. If no value is specified, the nearest feature at any distance will be determined.
column19: Determines whether the coordinates of the nearest point in the input and near feature will be added to the inputs attribute table.NO_LOCATION ĄŠThe coordinates are not added to the input feature. This is the default.LOCATION ĄŠThe coordinates are added to the input feature.
column23: Determines whether the horizontal arithmetic angle and vertical angle between the input feature and the nearest feature will be added to the input attribute table. NO_ANGLE ĄŠThe angles will not be added to the inputs attribute table. This is the default.ANGLE ĄŠThe horizontal arithmetic angle and vertical angle will be added to the NEAR_ANG_H and NEAR_ANG_V fields in the inputs attribute table.
column27: Determines whether the distances along the X, Y, and Z axes between the input feature and the nearest  feature will be  added to the input attribute table.NO_DELTA ĄŠNo distances will be added to the input attribute table. This is the default.DELTA ĄŠDistances along the X, Y, and Z axes will be calculated in the NEAR_DELTX, NEAR_DELTY, and NEAR_DELTZ fields.

column5: Merges closed, overlapping multipatch features from an input feature class.
column7: The multipatch features that will be unioned.
column11: The output multipatch feature class that will store the aggregated features.
column15: The field used to identify the features that should be  grouped together.
column19: A many-to-one table that identifies  the input features that contribute to each output.
column23: Specifies whether optimization is performed or not performed on the input data. Optimization will  preprocess the input data by grouping them to improve performance and create unique outputs for each set of overlapping features.ENABLE ĄŠOptimization is enabled, and the grouping field is ignored.  This is the default. This is the default.DISABLE ĄŠNo optimization is performed on the input data. Features will either be stored in a single output feature or be unioned according to their grouping field, if one is provided.
column27: Determines if the output feature class contains all features or only the overlapping ones  that were unioned.DISABLED ĄŠOnly unioned features are written to the output. Non-overlapping features will be ignored.ENABLED ĄŠAll input features are written to the output. This is the default.

column5: Generates 3D geometries  from existing 2D and 3D input features using rules authored in Esri CityEngine.
column7: Input polygon, or multipatch features.
column11: The CityEngine rule package *.rpk file containing CGA rule information and assets.
column15: The output feature class containing multipatch features with CGA rules applied.

column5: Exports ArcScene documents (.sxd) to Esri CityEngine Web Scene (.3ws) format to display them  in the CityEngine Web Viewer.
column7: The input ArcScene document (.sxd) to be exported to a CityEngine Web Scene.
column11: The output 3D Web Scene (.3ws) that will be created from the ArcScenedocument.

column5: Exports feature layers that have 3D properties defined to a multipatch feature class.
column7: The input feature layer that has 3D properties defined.
column11: The output multipatch feature class.
column15: The field in the input feature class that identifies the features that will be combined into the same multipatch feature.  The resulting attributes will be set to one of the input records.

column5: Exports 3D features to ASCII text files storing GENERATE, XYZ, or profile data.
column7: The 3D point, multipoint, polyline, or polygon feature class that will be exported to an ASCII file.
column11: The folder that output files will be written to.
column15: The name of the resulting ASCII file. If a line or polygon feature class is exported to XYZ format, the file name is used as a base name. Each feature will have a unique file output since the XYZ format only supports one line or polygon per file. Multipart features will also have  each part written to a separate file.  The file name will be appended with the OID of each feature, as well as any additional characters needed to make each file name unique.
column19: The format of the ASCII file being created.GENERATE ĄŠWrites output in the GENERATE format. This is the default.XYZ ĄŠWrites XYZ information of input features. One file will be created for each line or  polygon in the input feature.PROFILE ĄŠWrites profile information for line features that can be used in external graphing applications.
column23: The delimiter used to indicate the separation of entries in the columns of the text file table.SPACE ĄŠA space will be used to delimit field values. This is the default.COMMA ĄŠA comma will be used to delimit field values. This option is not applicable if the decimal separator is also a comma.
column27: The method used to determine the number of significant digits stored in the output files.AUTOMATIC ĄŠThe number of significant digits needed to preserve the available precision, while removing unnecessary trailing zeros, is automatically determined. This is the default.FIXED ĄŠThe number of significant digits is defined in the Digits after Decimal parameter.
column31: Used when the Decimal Notation is set to FIXED. This determines how many digits after the decimal are written for floating-point values written to the output files.
column35: The decimal character used to differentiate the integer of a number from its fractional part.DECIMAL_POINT ĄŠA point is used as the decimal character. This is the default.DECIMAL_COMMA ĄŠA comma is used as the decimal character.

column5: Creates polygon footprints representing the two-dimensional  area occupied by a multipatch feature class.
column7: The multipatch feature whose footprint will be generated.
column11: The resulting footprint polygon feature class.

column5: Imports 3D features from one or more ASCII files stored in XYZ, XYZI, or GENERATE formats into a new feature class.
column7: The ASCII files or folders containing data in XYZ, XYZI (with lidar intensity), or 3D GENERATE format. All input files must be in the same format.  If a folder is specified, the File Suffix parameter becomes required, and all the files that have the same extension as the specified suffix will be processed.
column15: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column19: The geometry type of the output feature class.MULTIPOINT ĄŠMultipoints are recommended the input data contains a large number of points and attributes per feature are not required.POINT ĄŠEach XYZ coordinate will produce one point feature.POLYLINES ĄŠThe output will contain polyline features.POLYGONS ĄŠThe output will contain polygon features.
column23: The factor by which Z values will be multiplied. This is typically used to convert Z linear units to match XY linear units. The default is 1, which leaves elevation values unchanged.
column27: The coordinate system of the input data. The default is an Unknown Coordinate System. If specified, the output may or may not be projected into a different coordinate system. This depends the whether the geoprocessing environment has a coordinate system defined for the location of the target feature class.
column31: The average planimetric distance between points of the input. This parameter is only used when the output geometry is set to MULTIPOINT, and its function is to provide a means for grouping the points together. This value is used in conjunction with the points per shape limit to construct a virtual tile system used to group the points. The tile systems origin is based on the domain of the target feature class. Specify the spacing in the horizontal units of the target feature class.
column35: The suffix of the files to import from an input folder. This parameter is required when a folder is specified as input.
column39: The decimal character used in the text file to differentiate the integer of a number from its fractional part.DECIMAL_POINT ĄŠA point is used as the decimal character. This is the default.DECIMAL_COMMA ĄŠA comma is used as the decimal character.

column5: Imports one or more 3D models into a multipatch feature class.
column7: One or more 3D models or folders containing such files in thesupported formats, which  are 3D Studio Max (*.3ds), SketchUp (*.skp), VRML and GeoVRML (*.wrl), OpenFlight (*.flt),  and COLLADA (*.dae).
column11: The multipatch that will be created from the input files.
column15: Indicates whether to produce one feature per file or one feature for every root node in the  file. This option only applies to VRML models.ONE_ROOT_ONE_FEATURE ĄŠThe generated output will contain one feature for each root node in the file.ONE_FILE_ONE_FEATURE ĄŠThe generated output will contain one file for each feature. This is the default.
column19: The coordinate system of the input data. For the majority of formats, this is unknown. Only the GeoVRML format stores its coordinate system, and its default will be obtained from the first file in the list unless a spatial reference is specified here.
column23: Identifies the axis that defines the vertical orientation of the input files.Z_IS_UP ĄŠIndicates that z is up. This is the default.Y_IS_UP ĄŠIndicated that y is up.
column27: The file extension of the files to import from an input folder. This parameter is required when at least one folder is specified as an input.* ĄŠAll supported files. This is the default.3DS ĄŠ3D Studio MaxWRL ĄŠVRML or GeoVRMLSKP ĄŠSketchUp 6.0FLT ĄŠOpenFlightDAE ĄŠCollada
column31: The point features whose coordinates define the real-world position of the input files. Each input file will be matched to its corresponding point based on the file names stored in the Symbol Field. The Coordinate System parameter should be defined to match the spatial reference of the points.
column35: The field in the point features containing the name of the 3D file associated with each point.

column5: This tool imports one or more triangulated irregular network (TIN) surfaces from a LandXML file to output Esri TINs.
column7: The input LandXML file.
column11: The folder that the output TINs will be created in.
column15: The basename of the resulting TIN. When several TINs will be exported from the LandXML file, the basename is used to define a unique name for each output TIN. If <basename> already exists, the tool will not write anything. If <basename> does not exist but <basename>2 exists, the tool will create <basename> and <basename>2_1, instead of <basename>2.
column19: The one or more LandXML TIN surfaces that will be exported to an Esri TIN. Each LandXML TIN can be specified by its name or  its index position in the LandXML file, where the number 1 represents the first TIN, 2 identifies the second, and so on.

column5: Creates multipoint features using  one or more lidar files.
column7: The LAS files that will be imported to a multipoint feature class. If a folder is specified, all the LAS files that reside therein will be imported.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The average 2D distance between points in the input file or files. This can be an approximation. If areas have been sampled at different densities, specify the smaller spacing. The value needs to be provided in the projection units of the output coordinate system.
column19: The classification codes to use as a query filter for LAS data points. Valid values range from 1 to 32. No filter is applied by default.
column23: The return values that will be used to filter the LAS points that get imported to multipoint features. ANY_RETURNS12345678LAST_RETURNS
column27: The LAS point properties whose values will be stored in binary large object (BLOB) fields in the attribute table of the output. If the resulting features will participate in a terrain dataset, the stored attributes can be used to symbolize the terrain. The Name column indicates the name of the field that will be used to store the specified attributes. The following LAS properties are supported:INTENSITYRETURN_NUMBERNUMBER_OF_RETURNSSCAN_DIRECTION_FLAGEDGE_OF_FLIGHTLINECLASSIFICATIONSCAN_ANGLE_RANKFILE_MARKERUSER_BIT_FIELDGPS_TIMECOLOR_REDCOLOR_GREENCOLOR_BLUE
column31: The coordinate system of the input LAS file.
column35: The suffix of the files to import from an input folder. This parameter is required when a folder is specified as input.
column39: The factor by which Z values will be multiplied. This is typically used to convert Z linear units to match XY linear units. The default is 1, which leaves elevation values unchanged.
column43: Scans through subfolders when an input folder is selected containing data in a subfolders directory. The output feature class will be generated with a row for each file encountered in the directory structure.NO_RECURSION ĄŠOnly LAS files found in an input folder will be converted to multipoint features. This is the default.RECURSION ĄŠAll LAS files residing in the subdirectories of an input folder will be converted to multipoint features.

column5: Generates statistical information about one or more point files in a polygon or multipatch output.
column7: One or more point data files or folders that will be analyzed.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The format of the input files.LAS ĄŠAirborne lidar format defined by the  American Society of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (ASPRS).XYZ ĄŠXYZ file.XYZI ĄŠXYZI file.GENERATE ĄŠGENERATE file.
column19: The suffix of the files to import when a folder is specified in the input. This parameter is required if an input folder is provided.
column23: The coordinate system of the input data.
column27: Scans through subfolders when an input folder is selected containing data in a subfolders directory. The output feature class will be generated with a row for each file encountered in the directory structure.NO_RECURSION ĄŠOnly the data found in the input folder will be used to generate the results. This is the default.RECURSION ĄŠAny data found in the input folder and its subdirectories will be used to generate results.
column31: Specifies whether to create a 2D polygon or multipatch feature class with extruded features that reflect the elevation range found in each file.NO_EXTRUSION ĄŠThe output will be created as a 2D polygon feature class. This is the default.EXTRUSION ĄŠThe output will be created as a multipatch feature class.
column35: The decimal character used in the text file to differentiate the integer of a number from its fractional part. DECIMAL_POINT ĄŠA point is used as the decimal character. This is the default.DECIMAL_COMMA ĄŠA comma is used as the decimal character.
column39: Specifies if the results will summarize LAS files per class code or LAS file.NO_ SUMMARIZE ĄŠEach output feature will represent all the class codes found in a lidar file. This is the default.SUMMARIZE ĄŠEach output feature will represent a single class code found in a lidar file.
column43: Provides enhanced assessment of the point spacing in LAS files that can reduce over-estimation caused by irregular data distribution.LAS_SPACING ĄŠRegular point spacing estimate is used for LAS files, where the extent is equally divided by the number of points. This is the default.NO_LAS_SPACING ĄŠBinning will be used to obtain a more precise point spacing estimate for LAS files. This may increase the tools execution time.

column5: Exports a triangulated irregular network (TIN) from a  LAS dataset.
column7: The LAS dataset to process.
column11: The TIN dataset that will be generated.
column15: Specifies the technique used for selecting a subset of LAS data points that would be exported to TIN.NONE ĄŠNo thinning is applied. This is the default.RANDOM ĄŠRandomly selects LAS data points based on the corresponding thinning_method selection and  thinning_value entry.WINDOW_SIZE ĄŠDivides the LAS dataset into square tiles defined by the thinning_value, then selects LAS points using the thinning_method.
column19: Specifies the technique used for reducing the LAS data points, which impacts the interpretation of the Thinning Value.  The available options depend  on the selected Thinning Type. PERCENT ĄŠThinning value will reflect a percentage of points in the LAS dataset.NODE_COUNT ĄŠThinning value will reflect the total number of nodes that are allowed in the output.MIN ĄŠLAS data point with the  lowest elevation in each window size areasMAX ĄŠSelects LAS data point with the highest elevation in each of the automatically determined window size areas.CLOSEST_TO_MEAN ĄŠSelects LAS data point whose elevation is closest to the average value found in the automatically determined window size areas.Specifies the technique used for reducing the LAS data points, which impacts the interpretation of the thinning_value.  The available options depend  on the selected thinning_type.PERCENT ĄŠ The thinning_value will reflect a percentage of the total points in the LAS dataset. This option is only available when thinning_type = RANDOM.NODE_COUNT ĄŠThe thinning_value will reflect the total number of nodes that are allowed in the output. This option is only available when thinning_type = RANDOM.MIN ĄŠSelects the LAS point with the  lowest elevation in each window size area. This option is only available when thinning_type = WINDOW_SIZE.MAX ĄŠSelects the LAS point with the highest elevation in each window size area. This option is only available when thinning_type = WINDOW_SIZE.CLOSEST_TO_MEAN ĄŠSelects the LAS point whose elevation is closest to the average value of all LAS points in each window size areas. This option is only available when thinning_type = WINDOW_SIZE.
column23: If  thinning_type = WINDOW_SIZE this value represents the sampling area of that the LAS dataset will be divided by. If thinning_type = RANDOM  and thinning_method = PERCENT, this value represents the percent of points from the LAS dataset that will be exported to the TIN. If thinning_type = RANDOM  and thinning_method = NODE_COUNT, this value represents the total number of LAS points that can be exported to the TIN.
column27: The maximum number of nodes permitted in the output TIN.  The default is 5 million.
column31: The factor by which Z values will be multiplied. This is typically used to convert Z linear units to match XY linear units. The default is 1, which leaves elevation values unchanged.

column5: Creates a polygon or polyline footprint of the data portions of a raster dataset.
column7: The raster to process.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The geometry of the output feature class.LINE ĄŠThe output will be a z-enabled line feature class.POLYGON ĄŠThe output will be a z-enabled polygon feature class.

column5: Converts raster cell centers into 3D multipoint features whose Z values reflect the raster cell value.
column7: The raster to process.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The  histogram table to be produced when VIP Histogram is specified for the Method parameter.
column19: The thinning method applied to the input raster to select a subset of cells that will be exported to the multipoint feature class. NO_THIN ĄŠNo thinning will be applied. This is the default.ZTOLERANCE <value> ĄŠOnly exports the cells that are required for maintaining a surface within the specified Z-range of the input raster.  The corresponding value reflects the maximum allowable difference in z units between the input raster and the surface created from the output multipoint feature class. When selecting this method, the thinning value defaults to one tenth of the z range of the input raster.KERNEL <value> ĄŠDivides the raster into equal sized tiles based on the thinning value, then selects one or two cells which meet the criteria defined by the designated kernel method. The corresponding value defaults to 3, which means the       raster would be divided into 3 cell by 3 cell windows.VIP <value> ĄŠEmploys a roving 3 cell by 3 cell window that is used to create a 3-dimensional best fit plane. Each cell is given a significance score based on its absolute deviation from this plane. A histogram of these scores is then used to determine the cells that get exported. The corresponding value reflects the percentile rank along the histogram of significance scores. This value defaults to 5.0, which means the cells whose score was within the top 5% of the histogram will be exported.VIP_HISTOGRAM ĄŠCreates a  table to view the actual significance values and the corresponding number of points associated with those values.
column23: The selection method used within each kernel neighborhood when kernel thinning is applied on the input raster.MIN ĄŠA point is created at the cell with the smallest elevation value found in the kernel neighborhood. This is the default.MAX ĄŠA point is created at the cell with the largest elevation value found in the kernel neighborhood.MINMAX ĄŠTwo points are created at the cells with the smallest and largest Z values found in the kernel neighborhood.MEAN ĄŠA point is created at the cell whose elevation value is closest to the average of the cells in the kernel neighborhood.
column27: The factor by which Z values will be multiplied. This is typically used to convert Z linear units to match XY linear units. The default is 1, which leaves elevation values unchanged.

column5: Converts a raster to a triangulated  irregular network (TIN) dataset.
column7: The raster to process.
column11: The TIN dataset that will be generated.
column15: The maximum allowable difference in (z units) between the height of the input raster and the height of the output TIN. By default, the z tolerance is 1/10 of the z range of the input raster.
column19: The maximum number of points that will be added to the TIN before the process is terminated. By default, the process will continue until all the points are added.
column23: The factor that the height values of the raster will be multiplied by in the resulting TIN dataset. This is typically used to convert Z units to match XY units.

column5: Converts a terrain dataset into a new point or multipoint feature class.
column7: The terrain dataset to process.
column11: The feature class that will be produced by this tool.
column15: The z-tolerance or window-size resolution of the terrain pyramid level that will be used by this tool. The default is 0, or full resolution.
column19: The name of  the terrain datasets embedded points to be exported.  If an embedded feature is specified, only the points from the feature will be written to the output. Otherwise, all points from all data sources in the terrain will be exported.
column23: The geometry of the output feature class.MULTIPOINT ĄŠThe output point features will be written to a multipoint feature class.  This is the default.POINT ĄŠThe output point features will be written to a point feature class.

column5: Interpolates a raster from  a terrain dataset.
column7: The terrain dataset to process.
column11: The location and name of the output raster. When storing a raster dataset in a geodatabase or in a folder such as an Esri Grid, no fileextension should be added to the name of the raster dataset. A file extension can be provided to define the rasters format when storing it in a folder, such as .tif to generate a GeoTIFF or .img to generate an ERDAS IMAGINE format file. If the raster is stored as a TIFF file or in a geodatabase, its raster compression type and quality can be specified using geoprocessing environment settings.
column15: The data type of the output raster can be defined by the following keywords:FLOAT ĄŠOutput raster will use 32-bit floating point, which supports values ranging from -3.402823466e+38 to 3.402823466e+38. This is the default.INT ĄŠOutput raster will use an appropriate integer bit depth. This option will round Z-values to the nearest whole number and write an integer to each raster cell value.
column19: The interpolation method that will be used to calculate cell values.LINEAR ĄŠApplies a distance based weight to the Z of each node in the triangle encompassing the center of a given cell, then sums the weighted values to assign the cell value. column5: Adds one or more feature classes to a terrain dataset.

column11: Identifies features being added to the terrain. Each feature must reside in the same feature dataset as the terrain and have its role defined through the following properties:Input Features ĄŠName of the feature class being added to the terrain.Height Field ĄŠField containing the features height information. Any numeric field can be specified, and Z-enabled features can also choose the geometry field. If the <none> option is chosen, Z-values are interpolated from the surface.SF Type ĄŠSurface feature type that defines how the features contributes to the terrain. Mass points denote features that contribute Z measurements; breaklines denote linear features with known Z measurements, and several polygon types. Breaklines and polygon-based feature types also have hard and soft qualifiers that define the interpolation behavior around the features edges when exporting to raster. Soft features exhibit gradual changes in slope, whereas hard features represent sharp discontinuities.Group ĄŠDefines the group of each contributing feature. Unspecification of breaklines and polygon surface features representing the same geographic features at different levels of detail are intended for display at certain scale ranges.  Data representing the same geographic features at different levels of detail can be grouped by assigning the same numeric value. For example, assigning two boundary features with a high and low level of detail to the same group would ensure there is no overlap in their associated display scale range.Min/Max Resolution ĄŠDefines the range of pyramid resolutions at which the feature is enforced in the terrain. Mass points must use the smallest and largest range of values.Overview ĄŠIndicates whether the feature is enforced at the coarsest representation of the terrain dataset. To maximize display performance, make sure that feature classes represented in the overview contain simplified geometry. Only valid for feature types other than mass points.Embed ĄŠSetting this option to TRUE indicates the source features will be copied to a hidden feature class that will be referenced by and only available to the terrain. Embedded features will not be directly viewable, as they can only be accessed through terrain tools. Only valid for multipoint features.Embed Name ĄŠName of the embedded feature class. Only applies if the feature is being embedded.Embed Fields ĄŠSpecifies Blob field attributes to be retained in the embedded feature class. These attributes can be used to symbolize the terrain. LAS attribution can be stored in Blob fields of multipoint features through the LAS To Multipoint tool.Anchor ĄŠSpecifies whether the point feature class will be anchored through all terrain pyramid levels. Anchor points are never filtered or thinned away to ensure theypersist in the terrain surface. This option only applies to single-point feature classes.

column5: Adds one or more pyramid levels to an existing terrain dataset.
column7: The terrain dataset to process.
column11: The z-tolerance or window size and its associated reference scale for each pyramid level being added to the terrain. Each pyramid level is entered as a space-delimited pair of the pyramid level resolution and reference scale (for example, 20 24000 for a window size of 20 and reference scale of 1:24000, or 1.5 10000 for a z-tolerance of 1.5 and reference scale of 1:10000). The pyramid level resolution can be provided as a floating-point value, while the reference scale must be entered as a whole number. The z-tolerance value represents the maximum deviation that can occur from the elevation of the terrain at  full resolution, whereas the window size value defines the area of the terrain tile used in thinning elevation points by selecting one or two points from the area based on the window size method defined during the creation of the terrain. The reference scale represents the largest map scale at which the pyramid level is enforced. When the terrain is displayed at a scale larger than this value, the next highest pyramid level is displayed.
column15: The pyramid type used by the terrain dataset. This parameter is not used in ArcGIS 9.3 and beyond, as its purpose is to ensure backward-compatibility with scripts and models written using ArcGIS 9.2.

column23: The configuration keyword for optimizing  the terrains storage in an enterprise database.
column27: The point thinning method used to construct the terrain pyramids.WINDOWSIZE ĄŠThinning is performed by selecting data points in the area defined by a given window size for each pyramid level using the criterion specified in the windowsize_method parameter.ZTOLERANCE ĄŠThinning is performed by specifying the vertical accuracy of each pyramid level relative to the full resolution of the data points.
column31: The criterion used for selecting points in the area defined by the window size. This parameter is only applicable when WINDOWSIZE is specified in the pyramid_type parameter.ZMIN ĄŠThe point with the smallest elevation value.ZMAX ĄŠThe point with the largest elevation value.ZMEAN ĄŠThe point with the elevation value closest to the average of all values.ZMINMAX ĄŠThe points with the smallest and largest elevation values.
column35: Specifies additional thinning options to reduce the number of points used over flat areas when Window Size pyramids are being used. An area isconsidered flat if the heights of points in an area are withinthe value supplied for the Secondary Thinning Threshold parameter. Its effect is moreevident at higher-resolution pyramid levels, since smaller areasare more likely to be flat than larger areas.NONE ĄŠNo secondary thinning will be performed. This is the default.MILD ĄŠWorks best to preserve linear discontinuities (for example,building sides and forest boundaries). It is recommended for lidarthat includes both ground and nonground points. It will thin thefewest points.MODERATE ĄŠProvides a good trade-off between performance and accuracy. It doesnot preserve as much detail as mild thinning but comes nearly asclose while eliminating more points overall.STRONG ĄŠRemoves the most points but is less likely to preserve sharplydelineated features. Its use should be limited to surfaces whereslope tends to change gradually. For example, strong thinning wouldbe efficient for bare-earth lidar and bathymetry.
column39: The vertical threshold used to activate secondary thinning with the WINDOWSIZE filter. The value should be set equal to or larger than the vertical accuracy of the data.

column5: Creates a triangulated irregular network (TIN) dataset.
column7: The TIN dataset that will be generated.
column11: The spatial reference of the output TIN.
column15: Add references to one or more feature classes that will be included in the TIN. For each feature class youll need to set properties that indicate how its used to define the surface.in_feature_class:   The feature class whose features will be imported into the TIN.height_field: The field that specifies the source of elevation values  for the features. Any numeric field in the features attribute table can be used. If the feature supports z-values, the feature geometry can be read by selecting the Shape.Z option. If no height is desired, specify the keyword <None> to create  Z-less features whose elevation would be interpolated from the surface.SF_type:  The surface feature type defines how the geometry imported from the features are incorporated into the triangulation for the surface. Options with hard or soft designation refer to whether the feature edges represent distinct breaks in slope or a gradual change when the triangulated surface gets converted to a raster. The following keywords are available: masspoints ĄŠElevation points that will be imported as nodeshardline or softline ĄŠBreaklines that enforce a height valuehardclip or softclip ĄŠPolygon dataset that defines the boundary of the TINharderase or softerase ĄŠ Polygon dataset that defines holes in the interior portions of the TINhardreplace or softreplace ĄŠPolygon dataset that defines areas of constant heighthardvaluefill or softvaluefill ĄŠPolygon dataset that defines tag values for the triangles based on the integer field specified in the tag_value columntag_value: The integer field from the attribute table of the feature class that will be used when the surface feature type is set to a value fill option. Tag fill is used  as a basic form of triangle attribution whose boundaries are enforced in the triangulation as breaklines. The  default option is set to <none>.
column19: Specifies the triangulation technique used along the breaklines of the TIN. DELAUNAY ĄŠThe TIN will use Delaunay conforming triangulation, which may densify each segment of the breaklines to produce multiple triangle edges. This is the default.CONSTRAINED_DELAUNAY ĄŠThe TIN will use constrained Delaunay triangulation, which will add each segment as a single edge. Delaunay triangulation rules are honored everywhere except along breaklines, which will not get densified.

column5: Redefines the data area, or interpolation zone, of a triangulated irregular network (TIN) based on its triangle edge length.
column7: The TIN dataset to process.
column11: The two-dimensional distance that defines the maximum length of a TIN triangle edge in the TINs data area. Triangles with one or more edges that exceed this value will be considered outside the TINs interpolation zone and will not be rendered in maps or used in surface analysis.
column15: The TIN edges that will be evaluated when delineating the TINs data area. PERIMETER_ONLY ĄŠIterates through triangles from the TINs outer extent inward and will stop when the current iteration of boundary triangle edges does not exceed the Maximum Edge Length. This is the default.ALL ĄŠClassifies the entire collection of TIN triangles by edge length.

column5: Adds  features from one or more input feature classes  that define the surface area of a triangulated irregular network (TIN).
column7: The TIN dataset to process.
column11: Specify features that will be included in the TIN. Each input can have the following properties set to  define the manner in which its features contribute to the surface: height_fieldĄŠThe field supplying elevation values for the features. Valid options include any numeric field and the Shape field, if the feature geometry supports Z-values. The <None> keyword is also available for features that do not have any qualifying fields or do not require elevation values. Z-less features have their values interpolated from the surrounding surface.tag_valueĄŠThe fill polygons that assign integer values to triangles as a basic form of attribution. Their boundaries are enforced in the triangulation as breaklines. Triangles inside these polygons are attributed with the tag values. Specify the name of the feature class attribute to use as tag values in the TIN. If tag values are not to be used, specify <none>.SF_typeĄŠThe surface feature type that defines how the feature geometry gets incorporated into the triangulation for the surface. Point features can only be used as masspoints, whereas line features can be defined  as breaklines, and polygons can be defined as the masspoints option, whereas lines can be defined as clip, erase, replace, and value fill features. Breaklines and polygon surface types have hard and soft qualifiers that indicate whether the features represent smooth or sharp discontinuties on the surface.use_zĄŠSpecifies whether Z values are used when the SHAPE field of the input feature is indicated as the height source.  Selecting true will result in Z values being used, and false will result in M, or measure values, being used. Zs are used by default.
column15: A constrained Delaunay triangulation conforms to Delaunay rules everywhere except along breaklines. When using conforming Delaunay triangulation, breaklines are densified by the software; therefore, one input breakline segment can result in multiple triangle edges. When using constrained Delaunay triangulation no densication occurs and each breakline segment is added as a single edge.DELAUNAY ĄŠ The triangulation will fully conform to the Delaunay rules. This is the default.CONSTRAINED_DELAUNAY ĄŠThe Delaunay triangulation will be constrained.

column5: Attributes features with spatial information derived from a  surface.

column15: The surface elevation property that will be added to the attribute table of the input feature class. The following list summarizes the available property keywords and their supported geometry types:Z ĄŠSurface Z values interpolated for the XY location of each single-point feature.Z_MIN ĄŠLowest surface Z values in the area defined by the polygon, along the length of a line, or among the interpolated values for points in a multipoint record.Z_MAX ĄŠHighest surface elevation in the area defined by the polygon, along the length of a line, or among the interpolated values for points in a multipoint record.Z_MEAN ĄŠAverage surface elevation of the area defined by the polygon, along the length of a line, or among the interpolated values for points in a multipoint record.SURFACE_AREA ĄŠ3D surface area for the region defined by each polygon.SURFACE_LENGTH ĄŠ3D distance of the line along the surface. MIN_SLOPE ĄŠSlope value closest to zero along the line or within the area defined by the polygon.MAX_SLOPE ĄŠHighest slope value along the line or within the area defined by the polygon.AVG_SLOPE ĄŠAverage slope value along the line or within the area defined by the polygon.
column19: Interpolation method used in determining information about the surface. The available options depend on the data type of the input surface:BILINEAR ĄŠAn interpolation method  exclusive to the raster surface which determines cell values from the four nearest cells. This is the only option available for a raster surface.LINEAR ĄŠ Default interpolation method for TIN, terrain, and LAS dataset. Obtains elevation from the plane defined by the  triangle that contains the XY location of a query point.NATURAL_NEIGHBORS ĄŠ Obtains elevation by applying area-based weights to the natural neighbors of a query point.CONFLATE_ZMIN ĄŠ Obtains elevation from the smallest Z value found among the natural neighbors of a query point.CONFLATE_ZMAX ĄŠ  Obtains elevation from the largest Z value found among the natural neighbors of a query point.CONFLATE_NEAREST ĄŠ Obtains elevation from the nearest value among the natural neighbors of a query point.CONFLATE_CLOSEST_TO_MEAN ĄŠ Obtains elevation from the Z value that is closest to the average of all the natural neighbors of a query point.
column23: The spacing at which z-values will be interpolated. By default, the raster cell size is used when the input surface is a raster, and the natural densification of the triangulated surface is used when the input is a terrain or TIN dataset.
column27: The factor by which Z values will be multiplied. This is typically used to convert Z linear units to match XY linear units. The default is 1, which leaves elevation values unchanged.

column35: Excludes portions of the surface that are potentially characterized by anomalous measurements from contributing to slope calculations. This is the default.AREA <value> ĄŠSurface triangles with 3D areas less than the specified value will be excluded from contributing to slope calculations.  LENGTH <value> ĄŠ  Line segments whose 3D length after being interpolated on the surface is shorter than the specified value will be excluded from contributing to slope calculations.
