column5: Adds raster datasets to a mosaic dataset from many sources, including a file, folder, table, or web service.
column7: The path and name of the mosaic dataset to which the raster data will be added.
column11: The raster type is specific for imagery products. It identifies metadata, such as georeferencing, acquisition date, and sensor type, along with a raster format. For a list of raster types, see the list of supported raster and image data formats.If you are using a LAS, LAS Dataset, or Terrain raster Type, an .art file must be used, where the cell size is specified.
column15: Path and name of the file, folder, raster dataset, mosaic dataset, table, or service.Not all input choices will be available. Those available will depend on the selected raster type.Dataset ĄŠAllows you to select an ArcGIS geographic dataset, such as any raster or mosaic dataset in a geodatabase or a table.Workspace ĄŠAllows you to select a folder containing multiple raster datasets. The folder can contain subfolders.This is affected by the Include Sub Folders and Input Data Filter parameters.File ĄŠAllows you to select one or more raster datasets stored in a folder on disk, an image service definition (.ISDef) file, and a raster process definition (.RPDef) file. Also, the file list  will ignore any files that do not correspond to the raster type being added. Do not use this with file formats that are raster datasets, such as TIFF or MrSID filesĄŠuse Dataset.Service ĄŠAllows you to select a WCS, map, or image service, or a web service layer file.

column19: Calculates the cell size ranges of each raster in the mosaic dataset. These values are written to the attribute table within the minPS and maxPS columns.UPDATE_CELL_SIZES ĄŠThe cell size ranges will be calculated for all the rasters in the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NO_CELL_SIZES ĄŠThe cell size ranges will not be calculated.
column23: Generates or updates the boundary polygon of a mosaic dataset. By default, the boundary merges all the footprint polygons to create a single boundary representing the extent of the valid pixels.UPDATE_BOUNDARY ĄŠThe boundary will be generated or updated. This is the default.NO_BOUNDARY ĄŠThe boundary will not be generated or updated.
column27: Defines and generates overviews for a mosaic dataset.UPDATE_OVERVIEWS ĄŠOverviews will be defined and generated.NO_OVERVIEWS ĄŠOverviews will not be defined or generated. This is the default.
column31: Defines the maximum number of pyramid levels that will be used in the mosaic dataset. For example, a value of 2 will use only the first two pyramid levels from the source  raster.  Leaving this blank or typing a value of -1 will build pyramids for all levels.This value can affect the display and the number of overviews that will be generated.
column35: Defines the maximum pyramid cell size that will be used in the mosaic dataset.
column39: Defines the minimum dimensions of a raster pyramid that will be used in the mosaic dataset.
column43: The spatial reference system of the input data.This should be specified if the data does not have a coordinate system; otherwise, the coordinate system of the mosaic dataset will be used. This can also be used to override the coordinate system of the input data.
column47: A filter for the data being added to the mosaic dataset. You can use SQL expressions to create the data filter. The wildcards for the filter work on the full path to the input data.The following SQL statement will select the rows in which  the following object IDs match.OBJECTID IN (19745, 19680, 19681, 19744, 5932, 5931, 5889, 5890, 14551, 14552, 14590, 14591)If you want to add only a TIFF image, add an asterisk before a file extension. *.TIFIf you want to add any image with the word sensor in the file path or the file name, add an asterisk before and after the word sensor.*sensor2009*Or you  can use PERL syntax to create a data filter.REGEX: .*1923.*|.*1922.*REGEX: .*19234567.*|.*194.*|.*195.* The following PERL syntax with multiple lexical groupings as part of the expression is not supported.REGEX: .* map_mean_.*(?:(?:a-z0-9*)_pptPct_(?:0-9|10-2*?)_20-9_*\\w*).imgAlternatively, you can use  the following syntax.REGEX: .*map_mean_*a-z0-9*_pptPct_(0-9|10-2)_20-9*_\\w*.img
column51: Recursively explores subfolders.SUBFOLDERS ĄŠAll subfolders will be explored for data. This is the default.NO_SUBFOLDERS ĄŠOnly the top-level folder will be explored for data.
column55: A check will be performed to see if each raster has already been added, using the original path and file name. This is the default.EXCLUDE_DUPLICATES ĄŠThe duplicate raster will not be added.OVERWRITE_DUPLICATES ĄŠThe duplicate raster will overwrite the existing one.
column59: Builds pyramids for each source raster.NO_PYRAMIDS ĄŠPyramids will not be generated. This is the default.BUILD_PYRAMIDS ĄŠPyramids will be generated.
column63: Calculates statistics for each source raster.NO_STATISTICS ĄŠStatistics will not be generated. This is the default.CALCULATE_STATISTICS ĄŠStatistics will be generated.
column67: Builds thumbnails for each source raster.NO_THUMBNAILS ĄŠThumbnails will not be generated. This is the default.BUILD_THUMBNAILS ĄŠThumbnails will be generated.
column71: A description you want used to represent this operation of adding raster data. It will be added to the raster type table which can be used as part of a search or as a reference at another time.
column75: Use the coordinate system that is specified for all the rasters when loading data into the mosaic dataset.NO_FORCE_SPATIAL_REFERENCE ĄŠKeep the coordinate system of each raster data when loading data. column5: Performs checks on a mosaic dataset for errors and possible improvements.
column7: The mosaic dataset you want to analyze.
column11: An SQL statement that confines your analysis to specific raster datasets within this mosaic dataset.
column15: Choose which parts of the mosaic dataset you want to analyze for known issues.FOOTPRINT ĄŠ Analyze the footprint geometry of each selected mosaic dataset item. This is checked on by default.FUNCTION ĄŠ Analyze the function chains for each selected mosaic dataset item.RASTER ĄŠ Analyze the original raster datasets. This is checked on by default.PATHS ĄŠ Check for broken paths. This is checked on by default.SOURCE_VALIDITY ĄŠ Analyze potential problems with the source data associated with each mosaic dataset item in the selected mosaic dataset. This is a good way to detect issues that may arise during synchronization workflows.STALE ĄŠ Overviews are stale when the underlying source data has changed. Once the mosaic dataset is analyzed, you can select which items are stale by right-clicking on the error and choosing Select Associated Items on the context menu.PYRAMIDS ĄŠ Analyze the raster pyramids associated with each mosaic dataset item in the selected mosaic dataset. Test for disconnected auxiliary files which can occur when they are stored in a raster proxy location.STATISTICS ĄŠ Test for disconnected auxiliary statistics files if they are stored in the raster proxy location. Analyze the covariance matrix associated with the raster, when the Gram-Schmidt pan-sharpening method is enabled. Analyze the radiometric pixel depth of a mosaic dataset item against the pixel depth of the mosaic dataset.PERFORMANCE ĄŠ Factors that increase performance include compression during transmission and caching items with many raster functions.INFORMATION ĄŠ Generate an information log for the analysis.

column7: Select the mosaic dataset where you want to recompute the boundary.
column11: An SQL Query to compute a boundary for select raster datasets. Use this option in conjunction with setting the append_to_existing parameter to APPEND to save time when adding new raster datasets.
column15: Set this to APPEND when adding new raster datasets to an existing mosaic dataset. Instead of calculating the entire boundary, APPEND will merge the boundary of the new raster datasets with the existing boundary.OVERWRITE ĄŠRecompute the boundary in its entirety.APPEND ĄŠAppend the perimeter of footprints to the existing boundary. This can save time when adding additional raster data to the mosaic dataset, as the entire boundary will not be recalculated. If there are rasters selected, the boundary will be recalculated to include only the selected footprints. This is the default.
column19: The simplification method reduces the number of vertices, since a dense boundary can affect performance.Choose which simplification method to use  in order to simplify the boundary.NONE ĄŠNo simplification method will be implemented. This is the default.CONVEX_HULL ĄŠThe minimum bounding geometry of the mosaic dataset will be used to simplify the boundary. If there are any footprints that are disconnected, then a minimum bounding geometry for each continuous group of footprints will be used to simplify the boundary.ENVELOPE ĄŠThe envelope of the mosaic dataset will provide a simplified boundary.  If there are any footprints that are disconnected, then an envelope for each continuous group of footprints will be used to simplify the boundary.

column5: Computes the extent of every raster in a mosaic dataset. This tool is used when you have added or removed raster datasets from a mosaic dataset and want to recompute the footprints.
column7: The mosaic dataset that contains the raster datasets whose footprints you want to compute.
column11: An SQL expression to select specific raster datasets within the mosaic dataset.
column15: Refine the footprints using one of the following methods:RADIOMETRY ĄŠ Exclude pixels with a value outside of a defined  range. This option is generally used to exclude border areas, which do not contain valid data. This is the default.GEOMETRY ĄŠ Restore the footprint to its original geometry.COPY_TO_SIBLING ĄŠ Replace the panchromatic footprint with the multispectral footprint when using a pan-sharpened raster type. This can occur when the panchromatic and multispectral images do not have identical geometries.NONE ĄŠDo not redefine the footprints.
column19: Exclude pixels with a value less than this number.
column23: Exclude pixels with a value greater than this number.
column27: Choose between 4 and 10,000. More vertices will improve accuracy but can extend processing time. A value of -1 will calculate all vertices. More vertices will increase accuracy but also the processing time.
column31: Clip the footprint by this distance. This can eliminate artifacts from using lossy compression, which causes the edges of the image to overlap into NoData areas.Shrinking of the polygon is used to counteract effects of lossy compression, which causes edges of the image to overlap into NoData areas.
column35: Use this parameter when using raster datasets that have been tiled and are butt joined (or line up along the seams with little to no overlap).NO_MAINTAIN_EDGES ĄŠRemove the sheet edges from all of the footprints. This is the default.MAINTAIN_EDGES ĄŠMaintain the footprints in their original state.
column39: Adjust the footprints of overviews.SKIP_DERIVED_IMAGES ĄŠDo not adjust the footprints of overviews. This is the default. NO_SKIP_DERIVED_IMAGES ĄŠAdjust the footprints of overviews and associated raster datasets.
column43: Update the boundary of the mosaic dataset if you have added or removed imagery that changes the extent.UPDATE_BOUNDARY ĄŠUpdate the boundary. This is the default.NO_BOUNDARY ĄŠDo not update the boundary.
column47: Set the resampled extent (in columns and rows) for the raster when building footprints. Greater image resolution provides more detail in the raster dataset but increases the processing time. A value of -1 will compute the footprint at the original resolution.
column51: Avoid small holes in your imagery when using pixel values to create a mask. For example, your imagery may have a range of values from 0ĻC255, and to mask clouds, youve excluded values from 245ĻC255, which may cause other, non-cloud pixels to be masked as well. If those areas are smaller than the number of pixels specified here, they will not be masked out.
column55: Reduce the number of vertices in the footprint to improve performance.NONE ĄŠDo not limit the number of vertices. This is the default.CONVEX_HULL ĄŠUse the minimum bounding box to simplify the footprint.ENVELOPE ĄŠUse the envelope of each mosaic dataset item to simplify the footprint.
column59: Snap the footprint to the sheet edge if it is within this tolerance. Units are the same as those in the mosaic dataset coordinate system. This is used when maintain_edges is set to MAINTAIN_EDGES.By default, the value is empty for which the tolerance is computed based on the pixel size corresponding to the requested resampled raster.A value of -1 will compute the tolerance using the average pixel size of the mosaic dataset.
column63: Identify all polygons that are smaller than the square of this value. The  value is specified in pixels and is based on the request_size, not  the spatial resolution of the source raster.Regions less than the (max_sliver_size)2 and also less than the min_thinness_ratio are considered slivers and will be removed.
column67: Define the thinness of slivers on a scale from 0ĻC1.0, where 1.0 represents a circle and 0.0 represents a polygon that approaches a straight line. Polygons that are below both the max_sliver_size and min_thinness_ratio will be removed from the footprint.

column5: Inserts the Cached Raster function as the final step in all function chains within a mosaic dataset.
column7: The mosaic dataset where you want to apply the cache function.
column11: An  SQL expression to select  specific raster datasets within the mosaic dataset on which you want the item cache built.
column15: Choose to define the mosaic dataset cache. A Cached  Raster function will be inserted to the selected items. If an item already has a Cached Raster function, it will not add another one.DEFINE_CACHE ĄŠThe Cached Raster function will be added to the selected items. If an item already has this function, it will not add another one. This is the default.NO_DEFINE_CACHE ĄŠNo raster cache will be defined.
column19: Choose to generate the cache files based on the properties defined in the Cached Raster function, such as the location and the compression of the cache.GENERATE_CACHE ĄŠCache will be generated. This is the default.NO_GENERATE_CACHE ĄŠCache will not be generated.
column23: Choose to overwrite the default location to save your cache. If the mosaic dataset is inside of a file geodatabase, by default the cache is saved in a folder with the same name as the geodatabase and a .cache extension. If the mosaic dataset is inside of an enterprise geodatabase, by default the cache will be saved inside of that geodatabase. Once created, the cache will always save to the same location. To save the cache to a different location, you need to first use the Repair Mosaic Dataset tool to specify a new location, and then run this tool again.  Once an item cache is created, regenerating an item cache to a different location is not possible by specifying a different cache path and rerunning this tool. It will still generate the item cache in the location where it was generated the first time. However, you can remove this function and insert a new one with the new path or use the Repair Mosaic Dataset tool to modify the cache path, then run this tool to generate the item cache in a different location.
column27: Choose how you want to compress your data for quicker transmission.LOSSLESS ĄŠ Retain the values of each pixel when generating cache. Lossless has a compression ratio of approximately 2:1LOSSY ĄŠ Appropriate when your imagery is only used as a backdrop. Lossy has the highest compression ratio (20:1), but groups similar pixel values to achieve higher compression.NONE ĄŠ Do not compress imagery. This will make your imagery slower to transmit, but faster to draw because it will not need to be decompressed when viewed.
column31: Set a compression quality when using the lossy method. The compression quality value is between 1 and 100 percent, with 100 compressing the least.
column35: Limit the size of the cache dataset by number of rows. If value is more than the number of rows in the dataset, the cache will not generate.
column39: Limit the size of the cache dataset by number of columns. If value is more than the number of columns in the dataset, the cache will not generate.
column43: Resample the cache using one of these two methods:PIXEL_SIZE_FACTOR ĄŠ Set a scaling factor relative to the pixel size. To not resample the cache, choose PIXEL_SIZE_FACTOR and set the request_size parameter to 1.PIXEL_SIZE ĄŠ Specify a pixel size for the cached raster.
column47: Set a value to apply to the request_size_type.

column5: Defines and generates overviews on a mosaic dataset.
column7: The mosaic dataset where you want to build overviews.
column11: An SQL statement to select specific rasters within the mosaic dataset.  The selected rasters will have their overview built.
column15: Identify where overviews are needed and define them.DEFINE_MISSING_TILES ĄŠAutomatically identify where overviews are needed and define them. This is the default.NO_DEFINE_MISSING_TILES ĄŠ Do not define new overviews.
column19: Generate all overviews that need to be created or re-created. This includes missing overviews and stale overviews.GENERATE_OVERVIEWS ĄŠGenerate all overviews, including those that already exist. This is the default.NO_GENERATE_OVERVIEWS ĄŠGenerate only the overviews that have been defined but not yet generated.
column23: Use if overviews have been defined but not generated.GENERATE_MISSING_IMAGES ĄŠGenerate overviews that have been defined but not generated. This is the default.IGNORE_MISSING_IMAGES ĄŠ Do not generate overviews that have been defined but not generated.
column27: Overviews become stale when you change the underlying raster datasets or modify their properties.REGENERATE_STALE_IMAGES ĄŠIdentify and regenerate stale overviews. This is the default.IGNORE_STALE_IMAGES ĄŠ Do not regenerate stale overviews.

column5: Generate or update seamlines for your mosaic dataset. Seamlines are used to sort overlapping imagery and produce a smoother-looking mosaic.
column7: Select the mosaic dataset on which to build seamlines.
column11: Generate seamlines for raster datasets that fall within the following range of spatial resolutions.  You can leave this parameter empty and the tool will automatically create  seamlines at the appropriate levels.The units for this parameter are the same as the spatial reference of the input mosaic dataset.
column15: Set a rule to determine which raster will be used to generate seamlines when images overlap.NORTH_WEST ĄŠ Select the raster datasets that have center points closest to the northwest corner of the boundary. This is the default.CLOSEST_TO_VIEWPOINT ĄŠ Select raster datasets based on a user-defined location and a nadir location for the raster datasets using the Viewpoint tool.BY_ATTRIBUTE ĄŠ Select raster datasets based on an attribute from the footprint attribute table. Commonly used attributes include acquisition date, cloud cover, or viewing angle.
column19: Choose whether to sort the rasters in ascending order or descending order.ASCENDING ĄŠ Sort the rasters in ascending order. This is the default.DESCENDING ĄŠ Sort the rasters in descending order.
column23: Order the raster datasets based on this field when the sort method is BY_ATTRIBUTE. The default attribute is ObjectID.
column27: Sort the rasters by their difference between this value and their value in the order_by_attribute  field.
column31: Set the coordinate location to use when sort_method  is CLOSEST_TO_VIEWPOINT.
column35: Choose how to build seamlines.GEOMETRY ĄŠ Generate seamlines for overlapping areas based on the intersection of footprints. Areas with no overlapping imagery will merge the footprints. This is the default.RADIOMETRY ĄŠ Generate seamlines based on the spectral patterns of features within the imagery.COPY_FOOTPRINT ĄŠGenerates seamlines directly from the footprints.COPY_TO_SIBLING ĄŠ Apply the seamlines from another mosaic dataset. The mosaic datasets have to be in the same group. For example, the extent of the panchromatic band does not always match the extent of the multispectral band. This option makes sure they share the same seamline.EDGE_DETECTION ĄŠ Generate seamlines over intersecting areas based on the edges of features in the area.The Sort Method parameter applies    to each computation method.
column39: Blending (feathering) occurs along a seamline between pixels where there are overlapping rasters. The blend width defines how many pixels will be blended.If the blend width value is 10, and you use BOTH as the blend type, then 5 pixels will be blended on the inside and outside of the seamline. If the value is 10, and the blend type is INSIDE, then 10 pixels will be blended on the inside of the seamline.
column43: Determine how to blend one image into another, over the seamlines. Options are to blend inside the seamlines, outside the seamlines, or both inside and outside.BOTH ĄŠ Blend using pixels on either side of the seamlines. For example, if the Blend Width is 10 pixels, then five pixels will be blended on the inside and outside of the seamline. This is the default.INSIDE ĄŠBlend inside of the seamline.OUTSIDE ĄŠBlend outside of the seamline.
column47: Specify the number ofcolumns and rows for resampling. The maximum value is5,000. Increase or decrease thisvalue based on the complexity of your raster data. Greater imageresolution provides more detail in the raster dataset but also increases the processingtime.
column51: Set the units for the Request Size.PIXELS ĄŠModify the request size based on the pixel size.This is the default option and resamples the closest image based on the raster pixel size.PIXELSIZE_FACTOR ĄŠModify the request size by specifying a scaling factor. This option resamples the closest image by multiplying the raster pixel size (from cell size level table) with the pixel size factor.
column55: Specify the unit of measurement for blend width.PIXELS ĄŠMeasure using the number of pixels. This is the default.GROUND_UNITS ĄŠMeasure using the same units as the mosaic dataset.
column59: SQL expression to build seamlines on specific raster datasets within the mosaic dataset.
column63: Build seamlines on all of the rasters that intersect this polygon. To select an area of interest, use an input feature class.
column67: Update new seamlines and regenerate existing seamlines that are affected by the addition or deletion of  the mosaic dataset items.IGNORE_EXISTING ĄŠRegenerates seamlines for all items and ignores existing seamlines, if any. This is the default.UPDATE_EXISTING ĄŠOnly update items without seamlines. If any new items overlap with the previously created seamlines, the existing seamlines may be affected. This parameter is ignored if seamlines do not exist.

column5: Computes the visibility levels of raster datasets in a mosaic dataset based on the spatial resolution.
column7: The mosaic dataset to calculate the visibility levels for.
column11: An SQL expression to select specific rasters in the mosaic dataset on which to calculate visibility levels.  If no query is specified, all the mosaic dataset items will have their cell size ranges calculated.
column15: Compute the minimum pixel size for each selected raster in the mosaic dataset.MIN_CELL_SIZES ĄŠCompute the minimum pixel size. This is the default. NO_MIN_CELL_SIZES ĄŠDo not compute the minimum pixel size.
column19: Compute the maximum pixel size for each selected raster in the mosaic dataset.MAX_CELL_SIZES ĄŠCompute the maximum pixel size. This is the default.NO_MAX_CELL_SIZES ĄŠDo not compute the maximum pixel size.
column23: Set a multiplication factor to apply to thenative resolution. The default is 10, meaning that an image with aresolution of 30 meters will be visible at ascale appropriate for 300 meters. The relationshipbetween cell size and scale is as follows: Cell Size = Scale * 0.0254 / 96 Scale = Cell Size * 96 / 0.0254
column27: Use this to group images with similar resolutions as the having the same nominal resolution. For example 1 m imagery and 0.9 m imagery can be grouped together by setting this factor to 0.1, because they are within 10% of each other.
column31: Calculate only the missing cell size range values.UPDATE_ALL ĄŠCalculate cell size minimum and maximum values for selected rasters within the mosaic dataset. This is the default.UPDATE_MISSING_ONLY ĄŠ Calculate cell size minimum and maximum values only if they do not exist.

column5: Makes transitions from one image to an adjoining image appear seamless.
column7: The mosaic dataset you want to color balance.
column11: The balancing algorithm to use. DODGING ĄŠChange each pixels value towards a target color. With this technique, you must also choose the type of target color surface, which affects the target color. Dodging tends to give the best result in most cases.HISTOGRAM ĄŠChange each pixels value according to its relationship with a target histogram. The target histogram can be derived from all of the rasters or you can specify a raster. This technique works well when all of the rasters have a similar histogram.STANDARD_DEVIATION ĄŠChange each of the pixels values according to its relationship with the histogram of the target raster, within one standard deviation. The standard deviation can be calculated from all of the rasters in the mosaic dataset or you can specify a target raster. This technique works best when all of the rasters have normal distributions.
column15: When using the Dodging balance method, each pixel needs a target color, which is determined by the surface type.SINGLE_COLOR ĄŠUse when there are only a small number of raster datasets and a few different types of ground objects. If there are too many raster datasets or there are too many types of ground surfaces, then the output color may become blurred. All the pixels are altered towards a single color pointĄŠthe mean (average) of all pixels.COLOR_GRID ĄŠ Use when you have  a large number of raster datasets, or areas with a large number of diverse ground objects. Pixels are altered toward multiple target colors, which are distributed across the mosaic dataset.FIRST_ORDER ĄŠ This technique tends to create a smoother color change and uses less storage in the auxiliary table, but it may take longer to process compared to the color grid surface. All pixels are altered towards many points obtained from the two-dimensional polynomial slanted plane.SECOND_ORDER ĄŠ This technique tends to create a smoother color change and uses less storage in the auxiliary table, but it may take longer to process compared to the color grid surface. All input pixels are altered toward a set of multiple points obtained from the two-dimensional polynomial parabolic surface.THIRD_ORDER ĄŠ This technique tends to create a smoother color change and uses less storage in the auxiliary table, but it may take longer to process compared to the color grid surface. All input pixels are altered toward multiple points obtained from the cubic surface.
column19: The raster that you want to use to color balance the other images. The balance method and color surface type, if applicable, will be derived from this image.
column21: Internet Tiled Layer;Map Server Layer;Raster Layer
column23: Applies a mask before color balancing the mosaic dataset. Create the mask using the Generate Exclude Area tool.
column27: Stretch the range of values before color balancing. Choose from one of the following options:NONE ĄŠ Use the original pixel values. This is the default.ADAPTIVE ĄŠ An adaptive prestretch will be applied before any processing takes place.MINIMUM_MAXIMUM ĄŠ Stretch the values between their actual minimum and maximum values.STANDARD_DEVIATION ĄŠ Stretch the values between the default number of standard deviations.
column31: Adjust the overall brightness of an image. A low value will minimize the contrast between moderate values by making them appear darker. Higher values increase thecontrast by making them appear brighter.
column35: The name of the field within a mosaic datasets attribute tableused to identify items that should be considered one item when performing some calculations and operations.

column5: Identifies areas within a mosaic dataset that have changed since a specified point in time. This is used commonly when a mosaic dataset is updated or synchronized, or when  derived products, such as cache, need to be updated. This tool will enable you to limit such processes to only the areas that have changed.
column7: The mosaic dataset that you want to analyze for changes.
column11: SQL expression to select specific rasters  within the mosaic dataset  on which to compute dirty areas.
column15: Compute the areas that have changed since the input time.XML time syntax:YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssYYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss.ssssZ2002-10-10T12:00:00.ssss-00:002002-10-10T12:00:00+00:00Non-XML time syntax:2002/12/2523:59:58.123
column19: The feature class containing the areas that have changed.

column5: Create  an empty mosaic dataset in a geodatabase.
column7: The path to the geodatabase.
column11: Name the mosaic dataset you are creating.
column15: The coordinate system  for all of the items in the mosaic dataset.
column19: The number of bands that the raster datasets in the mosaic dataset will have.
column23: Set the bit depth, or radiometric resolution, of the mosaic dataset. If not defined, it will be taken from the first raster dataset.1_BIT ĄŠA 1-bit unsigned integer. The values can be 0 or 1.2_BIT ĄŠA 2-bit unsigned integer. The values supported can be from 0 to 3.4_BIT ĄŠA 4-bit unsigned integer. The values supported can be from 0 to 15.8_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠAn unsigned 8-bit data type. The values supported can be from 0 to 255.8_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA signed 8-bit data type. The values supported can be from -128 to 127.16_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠA 16-bit unsigned data type. The values can range from 0 to 65,535.16_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA 16-bit signed data type. The values can range from -32,768 to 32,767.32_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠA 32-bit unsigned data type. The values can range from 0 to 4,294,967,295.32_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA 32-bit signed data type. The values can range from -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.32_BIT_FLOAT ĄŠA 32-bit data type supporting decimals.64_BIT ĄŠA 64-bit data type supporting decimals.
column27: Select a template that is either specific to the type of imagery you are working with, or is generic. The generic options are as follows:None ĄŠNo band ordering is specified for the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NATURAL_COLOR_RGB ĄŠCreate a three-band mosaic dataset, with red, green, and blue wavelength ranges. This is designed for natural color imagery.NATURAL_COLOR_RGBI ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset, with red, green, blue and near infrared wavelength ranges.FALSE_COLOR_IRG ĄŠCreate a three-band mosaic dataset, with near infrared, red, and green wavelength ranges.VECTOR_FIELD_UV ĄŠCreate a mosaic dataset displaying two variables.VECTOR_FIELD_MAGNITUDE_DIRECTION ĄŠCreate a mosaic dataset displaying magnitude and direction.DMCII_3BANDS ĄŠCreate a three-band mosaic dataset using the DMCII wavelength ranges.FORMOSAT-2_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the FORMOSAT-2 wavelength ranges.GEOEYE-1_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the GeoEye-1 wavelength ranges.IKONOS_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the IKONOS wavelength ranges.KOMPSAT-2_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the KOMPSAT-2 wavelength ranges.LANDSAT_6BANDS ĄŠCreate a six-band mosaic dataset using the Landsat 5 and 7 wavelength ranges from the TM and ETM+ sensors.LANDSAT_8BANDS ĄŠCreate an 8-band mosaic dataset using the  LANDSAT 8 wavelength ranges.LANDSAT_MSS_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the Landsat wavelength ranges from the MSS sensor.PLEIADES-1_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the PLEIADES-1 wavelength ranges.QUICKBIRD_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the QuickBird wavelength ranges.RAPIDEYE_5BANDS ĄŠCreate a five-band mosaic dataset using the RapidEye wavelength ranges.SPOT-5_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the SPOT 5 wavelength ranges.SPOT-6_4BANDS ĄŠCreate a four-band mosaic dataset using the SPOT-6 wavelength ranges.WORLDVIEW-2_8BANDS ĄŠCreate an eight-band mosaic dataset using the WorldView-2 wavelength ranges.WORLDVIEW-3_8BANDS ĄŠCreate an eight-band mosaic dataset using the WorldView-3 wavelength ranges.CUSTOM ĄŠDefine the number of bands and the average wavelength for each band.
column31: Edit the product_definition by adjusting the wavelength ranges, changing the band order, and adding new bands when using the CUSTOM product definition.

column5: Specify items from a mosaic dataset and create a separate mosaic dataset out of those items.
column7: The input raster catalog or mosaic dataset.
column11: The referenced mosaic dataset that you want to create.
column15: The projection for the output mosaic dataset.
column19: The number of bands that the referenced mosaic dataset will have.
column23: The bit depth, or radiometric resolution, of the mosaic dataset. If not defined, it will be taken from the first raster dataset.1_BIT ĄŠA 1-bit unsigned integer. The values can be 0 or 1.2_BIT ĄŠA 2-bit unsigned integer. The values supported can be from 0 to 3.4_BIT ĄŠA 4-bit unsigned integer. The values supported can be from 0 to 15.8_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠAn unsigned 8-bit data type. The values supported can be from 0 to 255.8_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA signed 8-bit data type. The values supported can be from -128 to 127.16_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠA 16-bit unsigned data type. The values can range from 0 to 65,535.16_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA 16-bit signed data type. The values can range from -32,768 to 32,767.32_BIT_UNSIGNED ĄŠA 32-bit unsigned data type. The values can range from 0 to 4,294,967,295.32_BIT_SIGNED ĄŠA 32-bit signed data type. The values can range from -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.32_BIT_FLOAT ĄŠA 32-bit data type supporting decimals.64_BIT ĄŠA 64-bit data type supporting decimals.
column27: An SQL expression to select raster datasets that will be included in the output mosaic dataset.
column31: Selects raster datasets based on the extent of another image or feature class. Raster datasets that lay along the defined extent will be included in the mosaic dataset. To manually input the minimum and maximum coordinates for the extent, use the Extent parameter.
column35: The minimum and maximum coordinates for the extent.
column39: Limit the extent to the shape or envelope when a feature class is specified in the in_template_dataset parameter.SELECT_USING_FEATURES ĄŠSelect using  the shape of the feature. This is the default.NO_SELECT_USING_FEATURES ĄŠSelect using the extent of the data within the feature class.
column43: A field in the raster catalog table defining the map scales at which the mosaic should be displayed; otherwise, a wire frame will be displayed.
column47: Specify a field from the footprint  attribute table that defines the minimum cell size for displaying the mosaic dataset; otherwise, only a footprint will be displayed.
column51: Specify a field from the footprint  attribute table that defines the maximum cell size for displaying the mosaic dataset; otherwise, only a footprint will be displayed.
column55: Set a maximum cell size to display the mosaic,  instead of specifying a field. If you zoom out beyond this cell size, only the footprint will be displayed.
column59: Rebuild the boundary. If the selection covers a smaller area than the source mosaic dataset, this is recommended.This is only available if the mosaic dataset is created within a geodatabase.BUILD_BOUNDARY ĄŠThe boundary will be generated or updated. This is the default. NO_BOUNDARY ĄŠ The boundary will not be generated.

column5: Specifies one or more values to be represented as NoData.
column7: The mosaic dataset where you want to update the NoData values.
column11: The number of bands in the mosaic dataset.
column15: Define values for each or all bands. Each band can have a unique NoData value defined, or the same value can be specified for all bands. If you want to define multiple NoData values for each band selection, use a space delimiter between each NoData value within the  bands_for_nodata_value parameter.The Mask function inserted by this tool is inserted before the Composite Bands function in the function chain. Therefore, if the function chain for each raster within the mosaic dataset contains the Composite Bands function, or if your raster data was added with a raster type that adds the Composite Bands function to each rasterĄŊs function chain, then any value you specify will apply to all bands.
column19: Specify a range of values to display for each band. Values outside of this range will be classified as NoData. When working with composite bands, the range will apply to all bands.
column23: An SQL statement to select specific raster in the mosaic dataset.  Only the selected rasters will have their NoData values changed.
column27: Choose whether all bands must be NoData in order for the pixel to be classified as NoData.NO_COMPOSITE_NODATA ĄŠIf any of the bands  have pixels of NoData, then the pixel is classified as NoData. This is the default.COMPOSITE_NODATA ĄŠAll of the bands must have pixels of  NoData in order for the pixel to be  classified as NoData.

column5: Lets you set how mosaic dataset overviews are generated. The settings you make with this tool are used by the Build Overviews tool.
column7: The mosaic dataset that you want to build overviews on.
column11: The folder or geodatabase to store the overviews.
column15: A raster dataset or feature class to define the extent of the overviews.
column19: Set the extent using minimum and maximum x and y coordinates. This is specified as space delimited in the following order: X-minimum X-maximum Y-minimum Y-maximum. The mosaic dataset boundary will determine the extent of the overviews if you do not define an extent.
column23: If you prefer not to use all the rasters pyramids, specify a base pixel size at which your overviews will be generated.The units for this parameter are the same as the spatial reference of the mosaic dataset.
column27: Specify the number of levels of overviews that you want to generate overviews. A value of -1 will determine an optimal value for you.
column31: Set the number of rows (in pixels) for each tile.Larger values will result in fewer, larger individual overviews, and increase the likelihood that you will need to regenerate lower level overviews. A smaller value will result in more, smaller files.
column35: Set the number of columns (in pixels) for each tile.Larger values will result in fewer, larger individual overviews, and increase the likelihood that you will need to regenerate lower level overviews. A smaller value will result in more, smaller files.
column39: Set a ratio to determine the size of the next overview. For example, if the cell size of the first level is 10, and the overview factor is 3, then the next overview pixel size will be 30.
column43: Generate overviews at all levels, or onlyabove existing pyramid levels.NO_FORCE_OVERVIEW_TILES ĄŠCreate overviews above the raster pyramid levels. This is the default.FORCE_OVERVIEW_TILES ĄŠ  Create overviews at all levels.
column47: Choose an algorithm for aggregating pixel values in the overviews.NEAREST ĄŠ The fastest resampling method because it minimizes changes to pixel values. Suitable for discrete data, such as land cover. If the Key Metadata Data Type is thematic, then nearest neighbor will be the default.BILINEAR ĄŠCalculates the value of each pixel by averaging(weighted for distance) the values of the surrounding 4 pixels.Suitable for continuous data.This is thedefault, unless the Key Metadata Data Type is thematic.CUBIC ĄŠ Calculates the value of each pixel by fitting a smooth curve based on the surrounding 16 pixels. Produces the smoothest image, but can create values outside of the range found in the source data. Suitable for continuous data.
column51: Define the type of data compression to store the overview images.JPEG ĄŠA lossy compression. This is the default, unless the Key Metadata Data Type is thematic. This compression method is only valid if the mosaic dataset items adhere to JPEG specifications.JPEG_YCbCr ĄŠA lossy compression using the luma (Y) and chroma (Cb and Cr) color space components.None ĄŠNo data compression.LZW ĄŠA lossless compression. If the Key Metadata Data Type is thematic, then nearest neighbor will be the default.
column55: Choose a value from 1 - 100. Higher values generate better qualityoutputs, but they create larger files.

column5: Deletes a mosaic dataset, its overviews, and its item cache from disk.
column7: The mosaic dataset that you want to delete.
column11: Delete all overviews associated with the mosaic dataset.DELETE_OVERVIEW_IMAGES ĄŠDelete the overviews associated with the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NO_DELETE_OVERVIEW_IMAGES ĄŠDo not delete the overviews.
column15: Delete the item cache associated with the mosaic dataset.DELETE_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠDelete the item cache associated with the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NO_DELETE_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠDo not delete the item cache.

column5: Adds, replaces, or removes a function chain in a mosaic dataset or a raster layer that contains a raster function.
column7: The mosaic dataset or a raster layer. If you use a raster layer, it must have a function applied.
column11: Determines if edits affect functions or the entire mosaic dataset.EDIT_MOSAIC_DATASET ĄŠEdits affect the functions associated with the mosaic dataset. This is the default. EDIT_MOSAIC_DATASET_ITEM ĄŠEdits affect the functions associated with all of the  items within the mosaic dataset.
column15: Insert, replace, or remove a function chain.INSERT ĄŠInsert the function chain above the Function Name of the existing chain. Specify the function chain in the location_function_name parameter. This is the default.REPLACE ĄŠReplace the existing function chain with the function chain specified in this tool. Specify the function chain below in the location_function_name parameter.REMOVE ĄŠ Remove the function chain starting from the function specified in the location_function_name parameter.
column19: Choose the function chain (rft.xml file) that you want to insert or replace.
column23: Choose where to insert, replace, or remove the function chain within the existing function chain.

column5: Creates a table of the file path for each item in a mosaic dataset. You can specify whether the table contains all the file paths or just the ones that are broken.
column7: The mosaic dataset containing the file paths to export.
column11: The table to create. The table can be a geodatabase table or a .dbf file.The SourceOID field in the output table is derived from the OID of the row in the original mosaic dataset table.
column15: An SQL expression to select specific rasters for export.
column19: Populate the table with either all of the paths, or only the broken paths.ALL ĄŠExport all  paths to the table. This is the default.BROKEN ĄŠExport only  broken paths to the table.
column23: Choose to export file paths from only the source raster, only the cache, or both. The default is to export all path types.RASTER ĄŠExport file paths from rasters.ITEM_CACHE ĄŠExport file paths from item cache.

column5: Masks pixels based on their color or by clipping a range of values. The output of this tool is used as an input to the Color Balance Mosaic Dataset tool to eliminate areas such as clouds and water that can skew the statistics used to color balance multiple images.
column7: The raster or mosaic dataset layer that you want to mask.
column11: The name, location and format for the dataset you are creating. When storing a raster dataset in a geodatabase, do not add a file extension to the name of the raster dataset. When storing your raster dataset to a JPEG file, a JPEG 2000 file, or a geodatabase, you can specify a Compression  type and Compression Quality within the Environment Settings.
column15: Choose the pixel depth of your input raster dataset. 8-bit is the default value; however, raster datasets with a greater bit-depth will need to have the color mask and histogram values scaled accordingly.8_BIT ĄŠThe input raster dataset has values from 0 to 255. This is the default.11_BIT ĄŠThe input raster dataset has values from 0 to 2047.12_BIT ĄŠThe input raster dataset has values from 0 to 4095.16_BIT ĄŠThe input raster dataset has values from 0 to 65535.
column19: Create your mask based on the color of the pixels or by clipping high and low values.COLOR_MASK ĄŠSet the maximum color values to include in the output. This is the default.HISTOGRAM_PERCENTAGE ĄŠRemove a percentage of high and low pixel values.
column23: The maximum red value to exclude. The default is 255.
column27: The maximum green value to exclude. The default is 255.
column31: The maximum blue value to exclude. The default is 255.
column35: The maximum white value to exclude. The default is 255.
column39: The maximum black value to exclude. The default is 0.
column43: The maximum magenta value to exclude. The default is 255.
column47: The maximum cyan value to exclude. The default is 255.
column51: The maximum yellow value to exclude. The default is 255.
column55: Exclude this percentage of the lowest pixel values. The default is 0.
column59: Exclude this percentage of the highest pixel values. The default is 100.

column5: Modifies the geometry for the footprints, boundary, or seamlines in a mosaic dataset to match those in a feature class.
column7: The mosaic dataset whose geometries you want to edit
column11: The geometry that you want to change.FOOTPRINT ĄŠThe footprint polygons in the mosaic dataset.SEAMLINE ĄŠThe seamline polygons in the mosaic dataset.BOUNDARY ĄŠThe boundary polygon in the mosaic dataset.
column15: The field in the mosaic dataset to use as a basis for the join.
column19: The feature class with the new geometry.
column23: The field in the input feature class to use as a basis for the join.

column5: Groups multiple items in a mosaic dataset together as one item.
column7: The mosaic dataset that has the items that you want to merge.
column11: An SQL expression to select specific rasters to merge  in the mosaic dataset.
column15: The field in the attribute table that you want to use to group images. Only date, numeric, and string fields can be specified as Block fields.
column19: Limits the number of items to merge. If the maximum is exceeded, the tool will create multiple merged items. The default is 1,000 rows.

column5: Removes selected raster datasets from a mosaic dataset.
column7: The mosaic dataset containing the rasters you want to remove
column11: An SQL expression to select the raster datasets that you want removed from the mosaic dataset.There must be a  selection or a query specified; otherwise, the tool will not run. If you want to delete all the records from the mosaic dataset,  specify a query that would select all the rasters, such as OBJECTID>=0.
column15: Updates the boundary polygon of a mosaic dataset. By default, the boundary merges all the footprint polygons to create a single boundary representing the extent of the valid pixels.UPDATE_BOUNDARY ĄŠThe boundary will be updated. This is the default.NO_BOUNDARY ĄŠ The boundary will not be updated.
column19: When the rasters in a mosaic catalog have been removed, any overviews created using those rasters may no longer be accurate; therefore, they can be identified so they can be updated or removed if they are no longer needed.MARK_OVERVIEW_ITEMS ĄŠThe affected overviews will be identified. This is the default.NO_MARK_OVERVIEW_ITEMS ĄŠThe affected overviews will not be identified.
column23: Remove overviews associated with the selected rasters.DELETE_OVERVIEW_IMAGES ĄŠDelete overviews associated with the slected rasters. This is the default.NO_DELETE_OVERVIEW_IMAGES ĄŠ Remove overviews associated with the selected rasters.
column27: Remove cache that is based on any source raster datasets that you are removing from the mosaic dataset.DELETE_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠRemove the item and its corresponding cache. This is the default.NO_DELETE_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠKeep the cache as part of the mosaic dataset.
column31: Remove item, cache, overviews, and raster datasets. Or, remove only the cache and overviews, and keep the raster datasets.REMOVE_MOSAICDATASET_ITEMS ĄŠRemove the item from the mosaic dataset.  This is the default.NO_REMOVE_MOSAICDATASET_ITEMS ĄŠRemove the item cache and any associated overviews, but not the item itself.
column35: Update cell size ranges for the mosaic dataset. Choose this option if you are removing all of the imagery at a specific cell size.UPDATE_CELL_SIZES ĄŠUpdate the cell size ranges. This is the default.NO_CELL_SIZES ĄŠDo not update the cell size ranges.

column5: Resets paths to source imagery if you have moved or copied a mosaic dataset.
column7: The mosaic dataset with the broken paths.
column11: A list of the paths to remap. Include the current path stored in the mosaic dataset and the path to which it will be changed. You can enter an asterisk (*) as the original path if you wish to change all your paths.
column15: An SQL expression to limit the repairs to selected rasters within the mosaic dataset.

column5: Defines the defaults for displaying a mosaic dataset and serving it as an image service.
column7: The mosaic dataset whose properties need to be set.
column11: The maximum number of rows for the mosaicked image, generated by the mosaic dataset for each request. This can help control how much work your server has to do when clients see your imagery. A higher number will create a more detailed image, but will also increase the amount of time to process the mosaic dataset. It is possible to set the value too small, and the image may not display.
column15: The  maximum number of columns for the mosaicked image, generated by the mosaic dataset for each request. This can help control how much work your server has to do when clients see your imagery. A higher number will create a more detailed image, but will also increase the amount of time to process the mosaic dataset. It is possible to set the value too small, and the image may not display.
column19: Define the method of compression for transmitting the mosaicked image from the computer to the display (or from the server to the client).None ĄŠNo compression will be used.JPEG ĄŠ Compresses up to 8:1 and is suitable for backdropsLZ77 ĄŠ Compresses approximately 2:1. Suitable for analysis.LERC ĄŠCompresses between 10:1 and 20:1. Compresses fast and is suitable for serving raw imagery with high bit-depth (12-bit to 32-bit)
column23: Set the default compression type. The default compression must be in the allowed_compressions list or already set in the mosaic datasets Allowed Compression Methods property.
column27: The compression quality when using JPEG. Compression quality ranges from 1 to 100.  A higher number means better image quality but less compression.
column31: Limits the per pixel  error when using LERC compression. This value is specified in the units of the mosaic dataset. For example, if the error is 10 centimeters and the mosaic dataset is in meters, enter 0.1.
column35: Determines how pixel values will be calculated when the dataset is displayed at small scales.  Choose an appropriate technique based on the type of data you have.NEAREST ĄŠThe fastest resampling method, and it minimizes changes to pixel values. Suitable for discrete data, such as land cover.BILINEAR ĄŠ Calculates the value of each pixel by averaging, based on distance, the values of the surrounding 4 pixels. Suitable for continuous data.CUBIC ĄŠCalculates the value of each pixel by fitting a smooth curve based on the surrounding 16 pixels. Produces the smoothest image, but can create values outside of the range found in the source data. Suitable for continuous data.MAJORITY ĄŠDetermines the value of each pixel based on the most popular value within a 3x3 window. Suitable for discrete data.
column39: Often the raster dataset and its footprint have the same extent. If they differ, you can clip the raster dataset to the footprint.NOT_CLIP ĄŠDo not clip  the raster to the footprint. This is the default.CLIP ĄŠClip the rasters to the footprint.
column43: Allow for pixels that have NoData values.FOOTPRINTS_MAY_CONTAIN_NODATA ĄŠShow pixels with NoData values. This is the default.FOOTPRINTS_DO_NOT_CONTAIN_NODATA ĄŠDo not show pixels with NoData values. Note that while you may see an improvement in performance, if your imagery does have NoData values, they will appear as holes in your mosaic dataset.
column47: Often the mosaic dataset and its boundary have the same extent. If they differ, you can clip the mosaic dataset to the boundary.CLIP ĄŠ Clip the mosaicked image to the boundary. This is the default.NOT_CLIP ĄŠDo not clip  the mosaicked image to the boundary.
column51: Enable color correction on the mosaic dataset.NOT_APPLY ĄŠKeep color correction off. This is the default.APPLY ĄŠUse the color correction that has been set up for the mosaic dataset.
column55: Choose which measurements your viewers can perform on the mosaic dataset. The ability to perform vertical measurements is dependent on your imagery and may require a DEM.None ĄŠNo mensuration capabilities will be allowed.Basic ĄŠ Allow ground measurements such as distance, point, centroid, and area calculations.Base-Top Height ĄŠAllow measurements from the base to the top of features. Rational polynomial coefficients must be imbedded within the imagery.Base-Top Shadow Height ĄŠAllow measurements from the base of a feature to the top of its shadow. Sun azimuth and sun elevation information is required.Top-Top Shadow Height ĄŠAllow measurements from the top of a feature to the top of its shadow. Sun azimuth, sun elevation, and rational polynomial coefficients are required.3D ĄŠA DEM must be available.
column59: Choose the default mensuration capability for this mosaic dataset. The default must be set in the allowed_mensuration_capabilities parameter list or already set in the mosaic datasets Mensuration Capabilities property.
column63: Define the rules for displaying overlapping imagery.None ĄŠOrder rasters based on the ObjectID in the mosaic dataset attribute table.Center ĄŠDisplay imagery that is closest to the center of the screen.NorthWest ĄŠDisplay imagery that is closest to the northwest corner of the mosaic dataset boundary.LockRaster ĄŠSelect specific raster datasets to display.ByAttribute ĄŠDisplay and prioritize imagery based on a field in from the attribute table.Nadir ĄŠDisplay the rasters with viewing angles closest to zero.Viewpoint ĄŠDisplay imagery that is closest to a selected viewing angle. Seamline ĄŠSmooth transitions between images using seamlines.
column67: Choose the default mosaic method for this mosaic dataset. The default must be set in the allowed_mosaic_methods parameter list or already set in the mosaic datasets Allowed Mosaic Methods property.
column71: Choose the default field to use when ordering rasters using the ByAttribute mosaic method. The list of fields is defined as those in the attribute table that are of type metadata and are integer. This list can include, but is not limited to:NameMinPSMaxPSLowPSHighPSTagGroupNameProductNameCenterXCenterYZOrderShape_LengthShape_AreaIf your field is a numeric or date field, then the order_base parameter needs to be set.This parameter is not needed if ByAttribute is not an allowed mosaic method.
column75: Sort the rasters based on their difference from this value in the field selected in the order_field parameterIf you are using a Date attribute, then it needs to be in one of the following formats:YYYY/MM/DD HH:mm:ss.sYYYY/MM/DD HH:mm:ssYYYY/MM/DD HH:mmYYYY/MM/DD HHYYYY/MM/DDYYYY/MMYYYY  This parameter needs to be specified only if ByAttribute is specified for the allowed_mosaic_method parameter.
column79: Choose whether to sort your rasters in an ascending or descending order.ASCENDING ĄŠSort ascending. This is the default setting.DESCENDING ĄŠSort descending.  This parameter needs to be specified only if ByAttribute is specified for the allowed_mosaic_method parameter.
column83: Define rules for resolving overlapping pixels.FIRST ĄŠDisplay the highest ranked image based on the mosaic method.LAST ĄŠDisplay the lowest ranked image based on the mosaic method.MIN ĄŠDisplay the lowest pixel values.MAX ĄŠDisplay the highest pixel values.MEAN ĄŠUse arithmetic mean to average overlapping pixels.BLEND ĄŠUse a distance weighted algorithm to average overlapping pixels.SUM ĄŠAdd all of the overlapping pixel values.
column87: Set the number of pixels over which to apply the BLEND mosaic_operator.
column91: When using the viewpoint mosaic method, you can horizontally shift the center of the image using this parameter. The units are the same as the spatial reference system.
column95: When using the viewpoint mosaic method, you can vertically shift the center of the image using this parameter. The units are the same as the spatial reference system.
column99: Set the maximum number of raster datasets that can be displayed at a given time in a mosaic dataset.
column103: Consider images of similar spatial resolutions as having the same nominal resolution. For example, with a factor of 0.1, all images with cell sizes within 10 percent of each other will be grouped together for tools and operations that use cell sizes.
column107: Set the cell size of the mosaic dataset using an existing raster dataset or specify its width (x) and height (y). If you specify the cell size, you can use a single value for a square cell size, or X and Y values for a rectangular cell size.
column111: Set the level of metadata to expose from the server to a client when publishing the mosaic dataset.FULL ĄŠTransmit metadata about processing applied at the mosaic dataset level as well as metadata related to the individual raster datasets.NONE ĄŠNo metadata will be exposed to the client.BASIC ĄŠTransmit metadata related to individual raster datasets, such as the number of columns and rows, cell size, and spatial reference information.
column115: Limit the fields in the attribute table that clients can view. By default, the list includes:NameMinPSMaxPSLowPSHighPSTagGroupNameProductNameCenterXCenterYZOrderShape_LengthShape_Area
column119: Enable animation through time on the mosaic dataset time aware. If time is activated, you need to specify the start and end fields, and the  time format.DISABLED ĄŠThe mosaic dataset will not be time aware. This is the default.ENABLED ĄŠThe mosaic dataset will be time aware. This allows the client to use the Time Slider.
column123: The field from the attribute table that shows the start time.
column127: The field from the attribute table that shows the end time.
column131: Select the format that represents time in the fields chosen above.YYYY ĄŠYearYYYYMM ĄŠYear and monthYYYY/MM ĄŠYear and monthYYYY-MM ĄŠYear and monthYYYYMMDD ĄŠYear, month, and dayYYYY/MM/DD ĄŠYear, month, and dayYYYY-MM-DD ĄŠYear, month, and dayYYYYMMDDhhmmss ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, and secondsYYYY/MM/DD hh:mm:ss ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, and secondsYYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, and secondsYYYYMMDDhhmmss.s ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, seconds, and fraction of secondsYYYY/MM/DD hh:mm:ss.s ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, seconds, and fraction of secondsYYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss.s ĄŠYear, month, day, hour, minute, seconds, and fraction of seconds
column135: The geographic transformations to associate with this mosaic dataset.
column139: Limit the number of raster datasets that can be downloaded per request.
column143: Limit the number of records that can be downloaded per request.
column147: Define the type of imagery in the mosaic dataset.GENERIC ĄŠThe mosaic dataset does not have a specified data type.THEMATIC ĄŠThematic data has discrete values, such as land cover. PROCESSED ĄŠThe mosaic dataset has  been color adjusted.ELEVATION ĄŠThe mosaic dataset contains elevation data.SCIENTIFIC ĄŠThe mosaic dataset contains scientific data.VECTOR_UV ĄŠThe mosaic dataset has two variables.VECTOR_MAGDIR ĄŠThe mosaic dataset has magnitude and direction.
column151: Set the minimum number of pixels required for a mosaic dataset item to be considered significant enough to be used in the mosaic dataset. Because of overlapping imagery, you may have an item that is displaying a small sliver of its overall image. Skipping these mosaic dataset items will help the performance of the mosaic dataset.
column155: Choose the function chains that you want to use to process a mosaic dataset or the mosaic dataset items, on-the-fly. You can add, remove, or reorder the function chains. All the template names that are added need to be unique.    For information on how to work with function chains, refer to Editing function chain templates  .
column159: Define the default function chain. The default function chain will be applied when the mosaic dataset is accessed.

column5: Splits mosaic dataset items that were merged together using Merge Mosaic Dataset Items.
column7: The mosaic dataset containing the items to split.
column11: An SQL expresseion to select items to split.If the query does not contain any previously merged items, the tool will return an error.

column5: Keeps your mosaic dataset up to date. In addition to syncing data, you can update overviews if the underlying imagery has been changed, generate new overviews and cache, and restore the original configuration of mosaic dataset items. You can also remove paths to source data with this tool. To repair paths, you need to use the Repair Mosaic Dataset Paths  tool.
column7: The mosaic dataset that you want to synchronize.
column11: An SQL expression to select which mosaic dataset items will be synchronized.  If an expression is not provided, all dataset items will be updated.
column15: Choose whether to  include new items when synchronizing, and then specify which options to update the new items with.If you choose to use this option, the items workspace will be searched for new data. When data is added to the mosaic dataset, it will use the same raster type as the other items within the same workspace.NO_NEW_ITEMS ĄŠNew items will not be added when synchronizing. This is the default.UPDATE_WITH_NEW_ITEMS ĄŠUpdate the mosaic dataset with new items in the workspaces. Optionally, you can  modify the existing items using OVERWRITE_EXISTING_ITEMS keyword in the  skip_existing_items parameter.
column19: Choose whether to update mosaic dataset items where the underlying raster datasets have been modified due to synchronizing. For example, building pyramids or updating the georeferencing of the rasters will affect how the overviews are rendered.SYNC_STALE ĄŠOnly update the  items where the underlying raster datasets have been modified. This is the default.SYNC_ALL ĄŠUpdate all of the items in the mosaic dataset.
column23: Choose whether to update the cell size ranges.UPDATE_CELL_SIZES ĄŠRecalculate the cell size for the entire mosaic dataset, but only for items that have an invalid visibility. This is the default.NO_CELL_SIZES ĄŠ Do not recalculate the cell size ranges.
column27: Choose whether to update the boundary.UPDATE_BOUNDARY ĄŠ The boundary will be rebuilt  after the mosaic dataset is synchronized. Use this option if syncing will change the extent of the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NO_BOUNDARY ĄŠ The boundary will not be rebuilt.
column31: Choose whether to update any obsolete overviews. The overview becomes obsolete if any underlying  rasters have been modified due to synchronizing.NO_OVERVIEWS ĄŠ The overviews will not be rebuilt. This is the default.UPDATE_OVERVIEWS ĄŠ The affected overviews will be rebuilt  after the mosaic dataset is synchronized.
column35: Choose whether to build pyramids for the specified mosaic dataset items. Pyramids can be built for each raster item in the mosaic dataset. Pyramids can improve the speed at which each raster is displayed.NO_PYRAMIDS ĄŠPyramids will not be generated. This is the default.BUILD_PYRAMIDS ĄŠ Pyramids will be generated for all the raster items that were updated due to synchronization.Pyramids will not be built for items that were added due to synchronization.
column39: Choose whether to calculate statistics for the specified mosaic dataset items. Statistics are required for your mosaic dataset when performing certain tasks, such as applying a contrast stretch.NO_STATISTICS ĄŠStatistics will not be calculated. This is the default.CALCULATE_STATISTICS ĄŠ Statistics will be calculated for the mosaic dataset items that were updated due to synchronization.Statistics will not be built for items that were added due to synchronization.
column43: Choose whether to build thumbnails for the specified mosaic dataset items. Thumbnails are small, highly resampled images that can be created for each raster item in the mosaic definition. Thumbnails can be accessed when the mosaic dataset is accessed as an image service and will display as part of the item description.NO_THUMBNAILS ĄŠNo thumbnails will be created or updated. This is the default.BUILD_THUMBNAILS ĄŠThumbnails will be generated or updated for all the raster items that were updated due to synchronization.Thumbnails will not be built for items that were added due to synchronization.
column47: Choose whether to build a cache for the specified mosaic dataset items. A cache can be created when youve added data using the LAS, Terrain, or LAS Dataset raster types. Items can also be cached using the Cached Raster function.NO_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠNo cache  will be created or updated. This is the default.BUILD_ITEM_CACHE ĄŠThe cache will be generated or updated for all the raster items that were updated due to synchronization.The cache will not be built for items that were added due to synchronization.
column51: Choose whether to rebuild the raster items from the data source using the original raster type.REBUILD_RASTER ĄŠRebuild the rasters from the source data.  You will lose any changes that you have performed on selected items in the mosaic dataset. This is the default.NO_RASTER ĄŠDo not rebuild the rasters. Other primary fields are reset if the update_fields parameter is set to UPDATE_FIELDS.This will only affect items that will be synchronized. This parameter is not applicable if the new_items parameter has been set to UPDATE_WITH_NEW_ITEMS.
column55: Choose whether to update the fields within the table. This will only affect items that will be synchronized.UPDATE_FIELDS ĄŠUpdate the fields from the source files.  This is the default.NO_FIELDS ĄŠDo not reset the fields within the table from the source.If you choose to update the fields, you can control which fields are updates by specifying them in the fields_to_update parameter. If you have made edits to some of the fields, you may want to remove them in the fields_to_update parameter.
column59: Specify which fields should be updated. This parameter is only valid if the option for the update_fields parameter  is set to UPDATE_FIELDS.If you have made edits to some of the fields, you may want to make sure they are not listed.The RASTER field can be refreshed, even if the REBUILD_RASTER  is not specified. However, if  REBUILD_RASTER is specified, the RASTER field is rebuilt, even if this field is not specified.
column63: Choose whether you would like to update existing items within your mosaic dataset.If you choose this option, you can then choose which existing parameters you would like to update: sync_only_stale, build_pyramids, calculate_statistics, build_thumbnails, build_item_cache, update_fields, or fields_to_update.UPDATE_EXISTING_ITEMS ĄŠExisting items will be updated with the parameters that you have chosen to update. This is the default.IGNORE_EXISTING_ITEMS ĄŠExisting items will not be updated.
column67: Choose whether you want to remove any broken links.Make sure that all your network connections are working properlyĄŠthis tool will remove any items that cannot be accessed.IGNORE_BROKEN_ITEMS ĄŠItems that have broken links will not be removed from the mosaic dataset. This is the default.REMOVE_BROKEN_ITEMS ĄŠItems that have broken links will be removed from the mosaic dataset.
column71: If  UPDATE_WITH_NEW_ITEMS  is specified in the new_items parameter then you can also choose whether to skip or to update  existing mosaic dataset items with the modified files from disk.SKIP_EXISTING_ITEMS ĄŠWhile adding new mosaic dataset items, the tool will not update existing mosaic dataset items. This is the default.OVERWRITE_EXISTING_ITEMS ĄŠWhile adding new mosaic dataset items, the tool will update existing mosaic dataset items that correspond to modified files on disk.
column75: Choose whether to re-include data that may have been removed from the mosaic dataset.NO_REFRESH_INFO ĄŠWhen synchronizing, do not include any rasters that may have been removed from the mosaic dataset. This is the default. REFRESH_INFO ĄŠWhen synchronizing, include rasters that may have been removed from the mosaic dataset.

column5: Makes a copy of a raster catalog, including all of its contents, or a subset of its contents if there is a selection.
column7: The name and location of the raster catalog to be copied.
column11:
The name and location of the output raster catalog.
You can copy your output raster catalog to any type of geodatabase: personal, file, or ArcSDE.
column15:
Specifies the storage parameters (configuration) for a file geodatabase and an ArcSDE geodatabase. Personal geodatabases do not use configuration keywords.

ArcSDE configuration keywords are set up by your database administrator.
column19: The Output Spatial Grid 1, 2, and 3 parameters apply only to file geodatabases and ArcSDE geodatabases. If you are unfamiliar with setting grid sizes, leave these options as 0,0,0 and ArcGIS will compute optimal sizes for you. For more information about this parameter, refer to the Add Spatial Index tool documentation
column23: Cell size of the second spatial grid. Leave the size at 0 if you only want one grid. Otherwise, set the size to at least three times larger than Spatial Grid 1.
column27: Cell size of the third spatial grid. Leave the size at 0 if you only want two grids. Otherwise, set the size to at least three times larger than Spatial Grid 2.

column5: Creates an empty raster catalog in a geodatabase
column7: The geodatabase to contain the raster catalog. This can be any type of geodatabase: personal, file, or ArcSDE.
column11: The name of the raster catalog to be created.
column15:
The coordinate system for the raster column in the raster catalog.

The spatial reference of the raster column is used during data loading as

A default spatial reference for those raster datasets that have an unknown spatial reference 
A target spatial reference if you choose to project your raster datasets that have different spatial references from the raster column 
The default value is the coordinate system set in the environment settings.
column19:
The coordinate system for the geometry column.

The spatial reference for the geometry column defines the spatial reference of the footprints of the raster datasets.
The default value is the coordinate system set in the environment settings.
column23:
Specifies the storage parameters (configuration) for a file geodatabase and an ArcSDE geodatabase. Personal geodatabases do not use configuration keywords.
ArcSDE configuration keywords are set up by your database administrator
column27:
The Output Spatial Grid 1, 2, and 3 parameters are used to compute a spatial index and only apply to file geodatabases and ArcSDE geodatabases. If you are unfamiliar with setting grid sizes, leave these options as 0,0,0 and ArcGIS will compute optimal sizes for you.

If you use the default spatial grid index (of zero), it is recommended that you load data using the Workspace To Raster Catalog tool. If that tool is used to load raster datasets, the spatial grid size will be automatically calculated. If another tool is used to load raster datasets into a raster catalog, the Calculate Default Spatial Grid Index tool needs to be used after the loading is completed.
For more information about this parameter, refer to the Add Spatial Index tool documentation.
column31: Cell size of the second spatial grid. Leave the size at 0 if you only want one grid. Otherwise, set the size to at least three times larger than Spatial Grid 1.
column35: Cell size of the third spatial grid. Leave the size at 0 if you only want two grids. Otherwise, set the size to at least three times larger than Spatial Grid 2.
column39:
Raster datasets within raster catalogs can be managed in two ways: managed or unmanaged (by the geodatabase).
MANAGED ĄŠWith a managed raster catalog, the raster datasets inside the raster catalog will be physically stored within the geodatabase. When a row (or raster) is deleted from the catalog, it is deleted from the geodatabase. 
UNMANAGED ĄŠWith an unmanaged raster catalog, the raster catalog only contains links or pointers connecting a row to a raster dataset stored outside the geodatabase. All raster datasets loaded into an unmanaged raster catalog must be a file on disk.
column43: If you want to base your new raster catalog on a template, you can specify a template raster catalog. The new raster catalog will then have the same fields as the template raster catalog.

column27: Choose whether the input 1-bit raster dataset will be converted to an 8-bit raster dataset. In this conversion the value 1 in the input raster dataset will be changed to 255 in the output raster dataset. This is useful when importing a 1-bit raster dataset to a geodatabase. One-bit raster datasets have 8-bit pyramid layers when stored in a file system, but in a geodatabase, 1-bit raster datasets can only have 1-bit pyramid layers, which makes the display unpleasant. By converting the data to 8 bit in a geodatabase, the pyramid layers are built as 8 bit instead of 1 bit, resulting in a proper raster dataset in the display.NONE ĄŠNo conversion will be done. column39: When the output is a different pixel type than the input (such as 16 bit to 8 bit) you can choose to have the values scaled to fit into the new range; otherwise, the values that do not fit into the new pixel range will be discarded. If scaling up, such as 8 bit to 16 bit, the minimum and maximum of the 8-bit values will be scaled to the minimum and maximum in the 16-bit range. If scaling down, such as 16 bit to 8 bit, the minimum and maximum of the 16-bit values will be scaled to the minimum and maximum in the 8-bit range.NONE ĄŠThe pixel values will remain the same and will not be scaled. column35: Use this option to create pyramids.For Pyramid Levels, choose a number of -1 or higher. A value of 0 will not build any pyramids, and a value of -1 will automatically choose the correct number of pyramid layers to create.The Pyramid Resampling Technique defines how the data will be resampled when building the pyramids.NEARESTĄŠNearest neighbor should be used for nominal data or raster datasets with color maps, such as land-use or pseudo color images.BILINEARĄŠBilinear interpolation is best used with continuous data, such as satellite imagery or aerial photography.CUBICĄŠCubic convolution is best used with continuous data, such as satellite imagery or aerial photography. It is similar to bilinear interpolation; however, it resamples the data using a larger matrix.The Pyramid Compression Type defines the method used when compressing the pyramids.DEFAULTĄŠThis uses the compression that is normally used by the raster dataset format.LZ77ĄŠA lossless compression. The values of the cells in the raster will not be changed.JPEGĄŠA lossy compression.NONEĄŠNo data compression.

column51: This is the origination location of the raster pyramid. It is recommended that you specify this point if you plan on building large mosaics in a file geodatabase or enterprise geodatabase, especially if you plan on mosaicking to them over time (for example, for updating).The pyramid reference point should be set to the upper left corner of your raster dataset.In setting this point for a file geodatabase or enterprise geodatabase, partial pyramiding will be used when updating with a new mosaicked raster dataset. Partial pyramiding updated the parts of the pyramid that do not exist due to the new mosaicked datasets. Therefore, it is good to set your pyramid reference point so that your entire raster mosaic will be below and to the right of this point. However, a pyramid reference point should not be set too large either.

column35: When mosaicking takes place, the target and the source pixels do not always line up exactly. When there is a misalignment of pixels, a decision needs to be made whether resampling takes place or whether the data should be shifted. The mosaicking tolerance controls whether resampling of the pixels take place or if the pixels should be shifted.If the difference in pixel alignment (of the incoming dataset and the target dataset) is greater than the tolerance, resampling will take place. If the difference in pixel alignment (of the incoming dataset and the target dataset) is less than the tolerance, resampling will not take place (instead, a shift is performed).The unit of tolerance is a pixel, where the valid value range is 0 to 0.5. A tolerance of 0.5 will guarantee a shift takes place. A tolerance of zero guarantees resampling, if there is a misalignment in pixels.For example, the source and target pixels have a misalignment of 0.25. If the mosaicking tolerance is set to 0.2, then resampling will take place since the pixel misalignment is greater than the tolerance. If the mosaicking tolerance is set to 0.3, then the pixels will be shifted.
column39: Choose the color matching method to apply to the rasters.NONE ĄŠThis option will not use the color matching operation when mosaicking your raster datasets.STATISTIC_MATCHING ĄŠThis method will use descriptive statistics from the overlapping areas; the transformation will then be applied to the entire target dataset.HISTOGRAM_MATCHING ĄŠThis method will match the histogram from the reference overlap area to the source overlap area; the transformation will then be applied to the entire target dataset.LINEARCORRELATION_MATCHING ĄŠThis method will match overlapping pixels and interpolate the rest of the source dataset; pixels without a  one-to-one relationship will use a weighted average.
